channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Growing comfortable with uncertainty
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
It’s almost a cliché that as one grows older, there are more questions than answers in one’s mind. We begin to accept that answers are transient, they shift and change with context and time. They are incomplete, with an uncertain edge and unclear tones. The deepest, most vexing questions are impossible to answer satisfactorily, defying neat responses. There are others that one cannot respond with more than “it depends”, a phrase that hangs in the air (or on the page/screen), invoking a mood like the start of a mystery novel.

Some time ago, I found many of the 20-somethings in my class restless and unable to focus on the tough text they had been asked to read that week, so I decided to ditch the discussion and instead open it up for a no-holds-barred conversation. It is placements season on my campus, and while a few had received offers of employment, the majority were looking anxiously at a hazy future. They were not sure if their degree would take them in the direction they had imagined; they were not sure if they had the skills the market demanded; and now, with AI entering the picture, they were not sure how long and to what extent they could hold the advantage of their education. As the adult in the room with some experience of that big world outside the shelter of the campus, they looked to me for answers, or at least some reassurance.

I was tempted to resort to comforting platitudes (“It will all work out.” “You’ll do fine.” “You’re all smart young people with a great future ahead of you.”). But even before I doled out the first one, I realized that wouldn’t do. They deserved honesty, not glibness. I needed to give them something to hold on to but also the assurance that they had – and could find – the internal resources to deal with challenges. It was also important that I first acknowledged their anxiety and recognized that their worries were legitimate, and attempt to share their view of the uncertainty facing them. Together, we had to learn to accept – perhaps even grow comfortable with – contingency and haziness, and to know that we’d never have a complete, defined, view of the future.

Facing uncertainty with younger students can be even tougher, as they may still think of the teacher as having all the answers. Indeed, they may come to depend on the idea that adults have certain knowledge of the world and can clearly see their way through life. Is there a way we can help young people get comfortable with uncertainty, and to not get overwhelmed when the path forward seems unclear? Can we model ways of saying “I don’t know” or “I’m not sure” while also emphasizing the need to constantly look for useful (not always complete) answers? How do you balance confidence and vulnerability? We’d love to hear your stories!
item.excerpt:encoded: It’s almost a cliché that as one grows older, there are more questions than answers in one’s mind. We begin to accept that answers are transient, they shift and change with context and time. They are incomplete, with an uncertain edge and unclear tones.

item.title: Opening it up with questions
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Manaswini Sridhar

As I looked at the sea of faces of the mid-level engineering managers, I wondered why these men (yes, they were all men!) needed someone to take them through language skills and soft skills. They appeared so confident and bright that I had my own misgivings about what I was going to bring to the table. I expected them to be bubbly and even aggressive, but they were reticent, almost bottled up. I had to pry and probe to find out how I was going to help them. But their silence did not deter me; I presented them with work scenarios they would probably have encountered and asked that they role-play them. After two or three sessions, the participants opened up, first a bit cautiously, and then when they discovered that I was not going to bite, they threw all caution to the wind, and posed questions about the various aspects of business English that seemed to elude them.

A week later, in the middle of a session, one of the participants suddenly commented, “We are quite at ease answering your questions because you don’t make us feel stupid if our answers are not correct; neither do you remark that we are asking silly or stupid questions. This is not the sense of comfort we are given in most of our training sessions.” I was flabbergasted that responsible adults could be slighted in sessions that are designed to build confidence and specific skills. With years of conditioning at school, and perhaps at home too, the anxious student in them continued to exercise control over them.

This takes me to a very fundamental question: Should we always pin the blame on the teacher, or should we, as parents, be appreciative of the many questions that children ask us, rather than dismiss them? Are we sending out signals that we don’t like being questioned, which is why children also become annoyed or cringe when asked questions?

As parents, are we listening to the answers with rapt attention or is there a twitch of impatience because the reply comes forth slower than anticipated and is certainly not the response we imagined? A child who is uncomfortable answering the parent will experience a big dose of peer pressure answering in class. Hence, shouldn’t parents be laying the groundwork for the classroom by opening their wards up to the world of questions and responses? Shouldn’t we be asking our children fundamental, open-ended questions such as, “What do you want to do?” in lieu of, “Do you want to colour or play?”, so that the machinery of the brain gets oiled?

If you have just read and talked about the story of The Hare and the Tortoise, engage the child with questions such as, “Why do you think Tortoise won? How do you think Hare felt?” As a parent, guide the child through the thinking process. Allow the child’s sparkling ideas to shine through and accept responses such as, “Tortoise had a healthy breakfast, whereas Hare stuffed himself with puris!” This kind of interaction will equip the child with language skills, and therefore bolster his confidence, allowing him to open up in class and think outside the box, unmindful of the 30 or 40 pairs of eyes.

Children need to be made aware that teachers ask questions not to judge them, but to evaluate whether a concept has to be retaught. They should also understand that they too can pose questions (politely) when they have not understood something; for all they know, this might benefit the whole class.

As teachers, we have been taught that the best way to engage students is to ask them questions. This gives the students the opportunity to talk, rather than just listen. Research[*] shows that teachers ask about 300 to 400 questions a day; this amounts to nearly 30% of their teaching time. Whether they are rhetorical questions, closed questions, open questions, or questions of the higher order or the lower order, the teacher’s singular aim is to see to it that students are actively participating. Additionally, it offers clues to the teacher of the students’ involvement or non-involvement in the process of learning.

We now come to a very relevant question: If questioning is such an integral part of schooling from day one, why is it that only a small percentage of students volunteer to answer while the others avoid having even eye contact with the teacher so that they are not ‘put on the spot’? What is it that is so formidable? Is it that the questions are difficult to understand because they are phrased vaguely? Is it the body language of the teacher that seems to be screaming impatiently, “You better answer this correctly!”? Is it the abrupt response of the teacher to an ‘incorrect’ answer? Or is it that there is no wait time between question and response?

Body language

A teacher needs to look relaxed and encouraging by showing interest on the face. Any sign of tension in the body or a raised voice can only result in fewer and more hesitant answers. It is mandatory to give eye contact (not stare or glare!) to all the students, especially students who hate being in the limelight.

When receiving an answer, the teacher must continue to maintain eye contact, and maybe give a slight nod to encourage the student to continue. Most of us think we are doing this, when we are actually not! Standing before the mirror and practising this can make a world of difference to a five-year-old who is unable to think straight because she feels that the angry-looking teacher is actually angry with her!

Kinds of questions and how to pose them

Different kinds of questions are meant to raise the classroom rigour. A lower-level question can be followed up with a probing question, challenging the students to analyze and think critically. Such questions will also help students apply what they have learned in the classroom to the outside world.

A lot is being discussed about higher order questions that help students develop higher order thinking skills. At the same time, it is important to ask lower order questions since they are simpler, and normally involve a one-word answer. Even shy or anxiety-prone students will not have too much difficulty with this. Lower order questions are also a way of refreshing the memory of students.

The hands-up approach in the classroom is what is traditionally followed, but both teachers and students know that only certain hands go up all the time; the remaining students generally hide in the shadows and just listen or pretend to listen. A better way would be to occasionally address the question to the entire class. By doing this, there are chances that the whole class participates.

The teacher may ask, “How many stanzas does the poem have?” It is essential for the students to be given a wait time of three to five seconds, depending on the complexity of the question. Wait time improves the quality of the students’ answers by giving them time to be more logical and relevant. The teacher too will not jump in and blurt out the answer. Some teachers intervene almost immediately because they find the wait time silence disconcerting. However, it is something that teachers need to get accustomed to. In this case, a two or three second wait time is adequate. Since the name of a student has not been referred to, the whole class, in likelihood, will be counting the number of stanzas in their book. At this point, the teacher can mention the name of a student who normally does not answer. Since it is an easy and quick one-word answer, most students will be at ease. The teacher can then turn to another student and ask, “Rahul, do you also think there are six stanzas?” If Rahul agrees, the teacher can continue with, “Which stanza do you think is different from the others?” Rahul may answer, “Stanza 2.” The teacher can continue with, “In what way is it different?” As the class is thinking, the teacher waits and then says, “Prithi, in what way is it different?” The simple straightforward questions have gradually turned into probing questions that require students to think. If Prithi is unable to answer, the teacher could continue with, “How many lines are there in stanza 1?” By adding, “Prithi, could you do a ‘think and share’ with your partner while the others do it with their partners too?”, the teacher continues motivating the students.

In this manner, students work together to peel the layers off the question to arrive at the answer. The question now becomes an activity for the whole class to participate in, and since the students have someone to bounce off their ideas on, they are surer of themselves. The teacher would be able to keep an eye on the quiet and shy students so that they are spurred on to discuss with their partners.

Probing questions are designed to have students compare and contrast anything else they might know or have read. The teacher could ask, “Does this poem remind you of another poem you have read this year? Write down the name of the poem on your sheet and hold it up. I shall give you a few seconds.” The teacher is likely to get a variety of answers since it does not involve oral communication. For those who think the students will waste their time tearing out pages from a notebook, it could become a practice for students to cut out strips of an appropriate size from a sheet of chart paper so that the teacher can read from a distance. Students could bring these from home every week.

Even a veteran teacher may not be able to come up with different levels of questions on the spot, so it is helpful to make a list of questions in advance for a particular lesson or period. This not only warrants that there are a variety of questions, but that the questions are formulated in a manner that is easy to understand. Most of the time, students are unable to answer because the question is either phrased vaguely or because the question itself has too many embedded questions, such as, “When did the First World War start? What were the causes and what was the result?” For a student grappling with facts, this can be daunting. So, it is pointless to repeat the question; obviously, the question needs to be rephrased.

The language used in asking questions should be targeted at the level of the students in class. The teacher may be teaching class 3, but the different sections of class 3 will require different modes of questioning. Some sections may require simple language, while in other sections it could be turned up a notch. Teachers need to remember that oral questioning is different from written questioning. A written question can be re-read, but most often oral questions are uttered only once.

When students give an answer without much thought, instead of accepting it, the student can be egged on with, “Shall we think about what you just said?” or, “Do you want to add to this?” The teacher could also say, “Could anyone add to this?” The teacher could wait a few seconds and look around the classroom as an indication that everybody is expected to participate.

Student doesn’t give the ‘correct’ response

If a student has made a mistake in answering a question, the teacher may be inclined to let off steam by giving negative feedback about the answer. (After all, the concept has just been revised!) However, this will result in the shy or anxious students to withdraw further into a shell because they are now even more convinced that mistakes are unacceptable, thus creating more obstacles for student learning. The teacher must hold up the motto in class that one learns by making mistakes, and not by avoiding them.

Encourage students to ask questions

Students find it stressful to answer questions, and they become even more anxious when they need to ask a question. This could be because they are shy or are afraid that they are asking something that all the other students already know. It could also be because they are not sure how to frame a question. Some other factors could be that the teacher has sometimes brushed aside questions saying that she will come to them later or made a tactless remark that it was dealt with just two days ago. True, it is difficult to delve into something again and again, but occasionally, another student could be asked to explain the answer. We must remember that teachers need not do the talking all the time.

The stress of asking questions can be brought down by making it a class activity. Once a lesson has been completed, the teacher can ask students to write down their questions about the lesson and hand them over collectively without mentioning their names. The teacher can then discuss these questions in class. If there is a question that has been repeated more than three times, it is also a message to the teacher that the concept or the idea has to be taught once again. As the teacher is reading out the questions, she can also make corrections in the construction of the question.

An activity to help with framing questions is for the teacher to say the answer. In mathematics class, the teacher could say, “The answer is 24. What is the question?” The students could be paired up and given three to five seconds, at the end of which, the students call out their questions. The variety of questions is likely to be a surprise. They could be as varied as: How many hours are there in a day? How many months are there in two years? What is 12 plus 12? What is 8 into 3? What is 72 divided by 3? If I have 8 novels, 12 comics, and 4 magazines, how many books do I have altogether? What is our room number? How many chairs are there before me? The combinations are endless, and sometimes very delightful. Students will be highly participative once they understand they cannot give a wrong answer… after all, they are framing the question. In this exercise, the teacher will probably hear the voices of those never heard before!

This kind of question quiz can extend to all subjects. If the teacher has just completed a lesson on Japan, she could say, “The answer is Japan. What is the question?” Students could have varied questions such as, “In which country is the kimono worn? Which country is known for the bonsai art? Where is Mount Fuji located? Which country is known as the Land of the Rising Sun?”

The students who have studied the text thoroughly are bound to ask more interesting questions. The task of the teacher is made simpler, and the students become more confident and driven.

When a student raises his hand to ask a question, the teacher too should take the time to give eye contact, nod and say, “Yes, Hari. You have something to say?” Once the question is asked, an encouraging, “Good question”, or something to that effect is a sure indication that the teacher does welcome genuine questions. A response to the question could at first be, “Would any of you be able to answer Hari’s question? Even a single word or a sentence will do, and I can add on.” This could motivate a student or two to give a gist of what they know. The student may not be right, but the teacher has succeeded in allowing students to feel less uneasy in a class situation.

When rounding up a topic or a section of a passage, instead of asking the more neutral but lukewarm, “Any questions?”, a warmer, “What questions do you have?” will be an indicator that having doubts is natural. Teachers should recognize that the words they use to ask questions are either going to elicit a response or a bored look. What is your teacher language?

When both teachers and students comprehend that questions and answers are not just academic skills, but life skills, they will work towards honing those skills more effectively. As someone said very picturesquely, “Don’t play a table tennis of questions in the classroom; play a basketball of questions.”

[*]Questioning the role of questions: new primary teachers’ realisations of over-reliance on questions in scientific dialogue, https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/25783858.2019.1659637#d1e377

Manaswini Sridhar is a teacher educator and language trainer based in Hyderabad. She can be reached at manaswinisridhar@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Manaswini Sridhar
Why do students hesitate to ask or answer questions in class? Manaswini Sridhar writes about small shifts in teaching and parenting that can encourage open dialogue.

item.title: Decoding creativity
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Earlier this week, in a workshop with a group of teachers, I asked them to write down values which they thought were essential for a teacher. In a matter of a few minutes a vast canvas was full of inspiring words like authentic, just, inclusive, open, punctual, clever, fun, crazy, political, and passionate. When asked to choose three values they consider core to their own teaching practice, a majority of teachers picked ‘creativity’ as one of their chosen three. Even those teachers, who at the start of the program, two years ago, had claimed that they didn’t consider themselves ‘creative’ had the word proudly written down.

This got me thinking. I firmly believe that everyone is creative, so surely, I can’t say that this was a case of transitioning from being non-creative to creative. What was this transition then? Perhaps, the only answer surfacing is that these teachers changed their perspective on what creativity meant, who is creative and what forms creativity can take. They redefined creativity, allowing them to see themselves as creative practitioners.

So how do we define creativity? The standard definition of creativity dating back to the 1930s said that creativity requires both originality and effectiveness. A definition that many scholars and philosophers adhere to even today. Originality is vital for creativity but not sufficient. Original things must be effective to be creative (Runco & Jaeger, 2012).This is interesting. A fork with a limp handle is of no use. Just like a class in which students colour ‘creative’ number worksheets with no idea that numbers carry values.

Recently, I observed a teacher in grade 3 taking an English class which was beautifully layered. It started with a simple game of blind man’s bluff that triggered rich conversations about the experience of not being able to see, bringing in personal experiences of engaging with visually impaired individuals. It then went into research tasks on famous people who were blind along with empathetic thinking tasks, considering changes they would have to make in their classroom if a blind child were to take admission in their school! All of this was an introduction to the story they were going to begin in English class on friendship, about a little girl and a visually-impaired boy.

At the end of the class, I expressed to the teacher how I was completely blown away by the seamless movement from one layer to the next and how the conversations created so much room to make varied connections, by the teacher and the students equally. The two were equally invested and curious about what was being discussed and I was amazed to see the entire class fully engaged, which is quite rare in our classrooms. She replied saying, “Earlier, I would be happy just getting them to draw a book cover for the story and be pleased with myself that I did something ‘creative’, but I now truly understand what creativity means.”

People who are creative are not afraid to question default methods, they are deeply reflective, they see the world differently, they are happy to experiment, take risks, and make mistakes. Malaguzzi stated that creativity was a way of thinking, knowing, and making choices. And when Ken Robinson talks about creativity, he talks about ‘putting your imagination to work’; he calls creativity, ‘applied imagination’.

I find the work I do fascinating. On the one hand, as part of my PhD journey, I read theories written by educators, philosophers, and scholars across several decades and various contexts. And on the other, I witness teachers playing out these very theories in their everyday lives, completely clueless about all this scholarship. Teachers sincerely put their creativity to work every single day. They use their own intuition. They are constantly thinking, knowing, and making choices in the little decisions they make and the everyday problems they solve, that perhaps goes unseen and unnoticed even by themselves.

There are several definitions of creativity, but what we can all agree on is that creativity is a process and the more we try to understand the HOW of that process, the more we can hone our creative abilities. This is what I have been trying to explore with teachers by breaking the process down to certain dispositions that we can identify and individually work on with the hope that creativity does not become a separate mental faculty but very much a part of how we think.

The disposition of playfulness builds one’s natural instincts of openness, of collaboration, of exploration, of risk taking, of problem solving, of embracing failure and uncertainty, of being aware of one’s verbal and physical communication, of persistence and determination, of fairness and justice, of being in a joyful space. All of this sharpens one’s creative thinking faculties.

How can teachers find opportunities to play regularly? The easiest way, quite obviously, is to bring play into the classroom and facilitate play with your students. But that’s not truly experiencing play. What is most essential is to build a culture of play as adults. Staff meetings can have 10-15 minutes at the start to play a quick game. Working Saturdays can have a 30-minute session where teachers can run off to the playground and decide a game, set rules, compete with each other, argue for fairness and really play! Short snatches of time to play within work hours is imperative to build this culture and disposition.

Understanding and investigating the nature of our own thinking is another habit that brings awareness to the process of thinking which can be enriched by asking the right kinds of questions, knowing in which direction to point and learning when and where to dig deeper. A math class on angles became threefold more interesting when the teacher asked the students to identify all the possible angles they could see in their classroom and then asked them to draw out all the angles that existed in their names for homework. Math is about recognizing patterns and this clearly brings the nature of the discipline to the forefront. It demonstrates that the teacher has spent time ‘thinking, knowing, and making choices’ in the questions they ask in the class – proving Malaguzzi right!

Making connections as a way of thinking creates opportunities for creative thinking to flourish. This is one of my most favourite dispositions to engage with and I’ve written about this at length in an earlier article as well titled Bringing the OUTSIDE inside the classroom. Educator and scholar, Koestler defines creativity as ‘the ability to make connections between previously unconnected ideas’. Doing this regularly as part of lessons not only enhances teachers’ creative thinking capacities but also models ways of thinking for the students they engage with. What we ‘study’ then, is not about the content dictated by the system, but an opportunity to connect with the world outside through it.

Building habits of ongoing reflection, spending time with one’s self to stay connected with our intuition, and to regularly engage with the creative arts to keep our sense of wonder and curiosity alive are ways in which we can engage all of our faculties to work together. Educator and researcher, Anna Craft, defines creativity in the most freeing way. “Creativity is a state of mind in which all of our intelligences are working together. It involves seeing, thinking and innovating. Although it is often found in the creative arts, it can be demonstrated in any subject at school or in any aspect of life.”

We have grown up around misconceptions of creativity. Those who are creative choose the arts, those who are not, must choose commerce or the sciences. It’s time to grow out of these notions. Medical practitioners are expected to be profoundly creative in order to find unique solutions to complex problems. Engineers, too, are constantly expected to re-invent. Circling back to what I said at the beginning, creativity is not seen only in “creative people”, everyone is creative. When you get home and cook up a meal with the bare minimum ingredients at the bottom of your refrigerator shelf, you are being creative.

I’d like to end this article with Anna Craft’s idea of Little ‘c’ Creativity coined to define everyday creativity:

  • as involving the active, conscious, and intentional taking of action in the world;
  • as a way of coping with everyday challenges, which may involve knowledge-based intuition just as much as step-by-step thought;
  • as involving innovation.

The focus here is on resourcefulness and agency of ordinary people, who identify problems and find innovative ways of coping with these everyday challenges. In the context of education, I find this way of defining creativity most empowering, where teachers reclaim their agency through self-determined processes, working on developing dispositions within themselves to hone their faculties towards creative thinking. They seek support in their peers and together teachers take ownership of HOW they think, know, and make decisions.

References

  • Koestler, A. (1999) The Act of Creation. Harmondsworth: Penguin.
  • Malaguzzi, L. (1996) The Hundred Languages of Children (exhibition catalogue) Reggio Emilia: Reggio Children.
  • Robinson, K (2016) Creative Schools: Penguin.
  • Craft, A. (2011) Creativity and Education Futures. Stoke-on-Trent: Trentham Books.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
What does it truly mean to be creative? Timira writes about how redefining creativity can transform teaching.

item.title: A teacher’s approach
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Have you ever noticed where your central focus is when you teach? Have your expectations ever affected the way things unfold?

You may understand what I mean if you hear this true story of a young yoga teacher in a residential school in South India.

Certified (and therefore hired) as a yoga teacher, she also donned two other roles: that of a houseparent and a volunteer for Kalaripayattu[*]. An expert would come every month from Kerala to teach Kalaripayattu, and this yoga teacher oversaw the practice of this martial art whenever the expert returned to Kerala. Why did she volunteer for this? Well, she had learnt karate, and Kalaripayattu is the most ancient marital art – in fact, it is considered to be the mother of all other forms of marital art. So her innate desire to learn this art form prompted her to volunteer to oversee students’ practice. Of course, she concurrently attended to her other duties: that of a houseparent as well as teaching yoga.

Pretty soon, she noticed a stark difference between the impact of yoga and Kalaripayattu. In the practice sessions of the latter, students wouldn’t mind staying on for hours, and would even be reluctant to leave the hall when practice ended. Whereas, when it came to yoga (the domain of her expertise), their lack of eagerness would be palpable in their body language as they ambled into her yoga classes. Students would often miss class or make up excuses to come late/leave early.

Why was this happening? It took her two long years to find the answer.

“I had entered Kalaripayattu classes out of my own volition. I did not bear the tag of an ‘expert’ nor did I hold rigid expectations. I just flowed along and enjoyed the practice sessions with the children. I too was learning along with them,” she declared. “But as a yoga teacher, I felt the need to control the way things went… to prove myself. I had certain set expectations: I thought I knew how things should proceed. You see, I was focusing more on myself in yoga than on the students!”

It was small wonder, then, that the students enjoyed Kalaripayattu more! No sooner did this realization hit than she grabbed the first opportunity to probe the students’ minds! In a substitution period, she asked them what they wanted from yoga. [“I had never done this before, you see! Because I thought I knew what they wanted!” she candidly confessed.]

To her surprise, they came out with very mature responses. Some wanted enhanced immunity, while others wanted to be more spiritual. Slowly, she began tailoring her yoga sessions so as to align them with the students’ expectations, and thus began a turnaround. Students began showing up for yoga even on exam days (when school rules allowed them to miss yoga!) They enthusiastically worked towards goals (like 36 Suryanamaskaras in a certain period of time) and what’s more, showed visible enjoyment.

Her obsessive concern with results and the need to prove herself slowly melted away as she slipped into enjoying yoga practice.

“Whenever I conduct a yoga class, nowadays, I remind myself that it is not about me, the teacher. It is for the students and they are my primary focus!”

As she began letting go of rigid expectations, naturally, so did her students. The buds of enjoyment began to blossom.

Ironically, she thus learnt the true meaning of yoga as taught by Sri Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita:

To action alone you have the right, and not to the fruits. Do not be impelled by the fruits of work. Nor have attachment to inaction. – Bhagavad Gita 2.47

Watch the video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LRPGaz8QpfU&t=14s

[*]Kalaripayattu is the oldest martial art in India – from Kerala – with a history of over 3,000 years.

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
A young yoga teacher learns how centering her teaching on students, instead of herself, started a transformative journey that helped her students rediscover the joy of learning.

item.title: From Pandora’s box to books: nurturing hope through children’s literature
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
JoAnne Saldanha

When Pandora opened the infamous box, releasing all the "evils" into the world, she swiftly closed it, hoping to undo the damage she had already done. I often recount this story to my students during our “myth” sessions in the library, imagining Pandora pausing for a few seconds, frozen in shock, as she realized what she had just unleashed. The evils whirled out like a storm, and in a frantic moment, she slammed the lid shut.

But then, a soft tap-tap-tap echoed from inside, a gentle sound urging Pandora to open the box once more. So compelling was its call that, despite the chaos she had just experienced, she could not resist. Aren’t we all grateful that she didn’t? If the myths are to be believed, that small, persistent sound was ‘hope’.

In many retellings of this story, we focus on the evils that Pandora unleashed, blaming her for the bad things we encounter in the world. However, perhaps Pandora deserves more credit for pushing past her fear and taking that leap of faith to reopen the box. The lesson in the story is clear: hope is something that endures, quietly striving to be free and to thrive, no matter the odds. It embodies resilience in its truest form.

Hope vs. wishes: a subtle difference

We often use the words hope and wish interchangeably, but they are fundamentally different. In her article “Is Hope Really a Thing with Feathers?” in Teacher Plus, Lakshmi Karunakaran writes, “Hope is fundamentally different from, say, a wish. When we wish, we desire a particular outcome but lack the plan or willpower to make it a reality. Hope, on the other hand, is accessible to most of us. While a goal can be easily imagined, the creation of pathways – and the willpower to walk them – is something many, especially children, struggle with.”

In The Lord of the Rings, J.R.R. Tolkien wrote, “Where there’s life, there’s hope.”This truth holds especially strong today, as we live in challenging and often confusing times. Now, imagine how much more bewildering this world must seem to children, who are constantly bombarded by media filled with doom and gloom.

This is where stories come in. Stories remind us that hope is a fundamental human quality, and they show us that hope and action go hand in hand. As author Onjali Q. Rauf beautifully puts it, “There is one place where every child can go, to find comfort, happiness, and the inspiration they need to build the kind of world they dream of. And that is, of course, the glorious, endless domain of books!”

Children’s literature offers an imaginative space where anything is possible. It shows young readers that another world – one full of hope and possibility – is within reach, all while honing their creativity and critical thinking skills. Imagination and hope are intrinsically connected, as it is imagination that gives us the tools to create pathways and the agency needed to act, as Karunakaran so insightfully points out.

As humans, we call on hope in so many different situations – hope for the future, for change, for growth. Let’s explore how this theme of hope appears in children’s literature, and how these stories help shape the way children understand the world and their role in it.

Everyday hope

Hope is often difficult to define, yet we all carry an innate desire for it – a simple wish for a good day. At the heart of this hope is the universal need to be “seen.” In the metaphorical sense, How Full Is Your Bucket? For Kids teaches us that when we choose to show kindness and recognize others, we not only fill their emotional buckets but our own as well.

In Each Kindness, children witness the power of their actions and the profound effect they have on others’ feelings, demonstrating how our interactions shape both their hopes and our own.

Dr. Seuss captures the essence of hope with his signature rhyme and wisdom, in Oh, The Places You’ll Go!, offering comfort to children – and adults alike. He encourages us to follow our hearts while also teaching us to be brave in the face of unexpected challenges. The book becomes a guide, providing both reassurance and the courage needed to navigate life's uncertainties.

In The Nameless God, Bachu and Noor begin with the simple hope for a holiday from school, but when their world is torn apart by terror and violence, their journey reveals a deeper, more powerful hope. Through acts of kindness and resilience, they find that even in the face of evil, good still exists. Their story reminds us that hope often grows in the darkest of times, fuelled by the kindness and goodness we share with others.

Hope to be accepted for who you are

In My Name is Gulab, young Gulab hopes to erase the stigma attached to her caste. Driven by the desire to improve the life of her father, a manual scavenger, and that of her family, she ‘invents’ a machine to do his work. Although her invention is just an idea, it powerfully represents her action toward realizing the hope she holds for a better future. In The Boy Who Asked Why, a young Dr. B.R. Ambedkar dreams of a society founded on equality. His constant questioning of the status quo set him on a lifelong path of struggle for social justice – a symbol of hope for many, even today. A simple yet profound hope emerges in Why Are You Afraid to Hold My Hand?, the hope and desire for every child to be treated with dignity and respect, regardless of their abilities. Similarly, in Guthli Has Wings, Guthli, a transgender child, hopes her family will see her as she sees herself. Cricket is a means for Loki to realize her hopes and dreams in Loki takes Guard. Her journey is a testament to the power of resilience and hope, facing challenges and embracing victories with grace.

Good times follow the bad: the hope of resilience

The belief that good times will follow the bad is deeply ingrained in our religious and philosophical traditions, conditioning us to push through tough times with the firm conviction that after every storm, a brighter day awaits. Rain before Rainbows serves as a reminder of our enduring faith, offering reassurance when we need it most. “All rainstorms must end, and this rainstorm will too”, was the message sent out by children who painted rainbows to display in their windows during the pandemic to remind passersby that the world is always full of hope and possibilities. The World Made a Rainbow captures this sentiment.

Hope for a better world

One of the most enduring collective hopes is the desire for a better world. In light of the overwhelming statistics on global warming and climate change, and the dire warnings that often feel like threats, it’s easy to feel discouraged and resigned to a sense of “what will be, will be.” However, the truth is that responsibility lies with each of us. Along with our hope for a better world, we must take action. To act, we must first help children develop a deep love for the wonders of nature. Once they appreciate its beauty, they can understand that even the smallest actions contribute to meaningful change. Books like Go Wild nurture that sense of wonder and respect for nature, while We Hope: Children on Climate Change gives voice to the concerns and hopes of children across India. This book reflects their despair about the effects of climate change on their lives while offering insight into how we can make a difference and impact the future of our planet.

Hope in the power of communities

The hope for a better world extends beyond the physical environment; it also encompasses a utopian vision – a collective hope for a brighter future. This hope thrives when we come together as communities, finding strength and solidarity in our shared aspirations. Art is a Voice encourages readers to act on this hope by first caring, becoming aware, connecting with others, and contributing to making those hopes a reality. Young Sarojini in Dear Mrs. Naidu, displays just this, drawing together a community of children and adults who realize that their actions, big or small could contribute to social change. Change Sings beautifully illustrates this collective power, lyrically…

"I hum with a hundred hearts,
Each of us lifting a hand.
I use my strengths and my smarts,
Take a knee to make a stand."

The Miracle on Sunderbaag Street and The Miracle on Kacchua Beach, highlight how action and community work together, impacting not only our immediate surroundings but also our sense of wellbeing.

Grieving the village he left behind in Pakistan during the Partition, Nek Chand channels his sorrow into the creation of a fantastical rock garden, building it Rock by Rock as a way to heal. When the garden is discovered and threatened with destruction, the community unites in a collective effort to protect his creation, showing the power of solidarity in preserving hope and beauty.

In Behind the Lie, compassionate community members bring smiles and hope to a mother and her children. Sometimes, all it takes is knowing your neighbours and ‘seeing’ them, to notice when something is wrong and offer the support and hope they need.

Hope that pushes through adversity

Picking yourself up after a fall, physical or metaphorical, is always testing. We often hope to get back to all we did before, yet it requires determination and action to pick ourselves up and take that first step. Humpty Dumpty…yes, the same one who ‘had a great fall’, illustrates just how to pick yourself up in After the Fall.

In Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry, Casey Logan and her family attempt to gain their own land and forge a path for themselves in a world determined to keep them down.

While a story of unrelenting hope is illustrated in ‘When Stars are Scattered’. Set in one of the most despairing environments…a refugee camp, it is the true story of Omar Mohamed’s childhood in a refugee camp after fleeing the civil war in Somalia and his efforts to keep his family together.

In the wordless picture book Migrants, the dark background shows the bleakness of the migrants having to leave behind their homes as they travel across forests and seas, to try and find a new home where they are welcome. The colour used for the characters against the dark background speaks of the hope they carry with them as they walk in uncertainty.

Stories from history and historical fiction often unfold in liminal spaces – moments of transition that can feel dark and frightening. However, by experiencing these transitions through stories like those in the Songs of Freedom series, we learn that life continues, that things improve, and, ultimately, we discover hope.

Mukund and Riaz and Roop and the River Crossing, tell us that we find hope in each other, sometimes it could be just an individual who is the conduit to our hope.

“Real, true hope isn’t the promise that everything will be all right – but it’s a belief that the world has so many strangenesses and possibilities that giving up would be a mistake; that we live in a universe shot with the unexpected” writes Katherine Rundell, in the prologue to her The Book of Hopes.

Both fiction and nonfiction books convey messages of hope. Fiction often presents a plot where challenges are overcome, while nonfiction highlights real-life actions people take to confront and resolve difficulties. These works illustrate hope through stories of perseverance, problem-solving, and the proactive steps we can take to navigate adversity.

“The value of children’s literature cannot, of course, be predicated on pain – books are not only valuable when we have something to escape from – but in fact those books were not an escape. They said: look, this is what bravery looks like. This is what hope looks like. They told me, through the medium of wizards and lions and babysitters and talking spiders, that this world we live in is a world of people who tell jokes and work and endure. Children’s books say: the world is huge. They say: hope counts for something. They say: bravery will matter, wit will matter, empathy will matter, love will matter.” – Katherine Rundell

Books listed in this article:

  1. How Full is Your Bucket? For Kids by Mary Reckmeyer, Tom Rath/Gallup Press
  2. Each Kindness by Jaqueline Woodsen/E.B.Lewis/Nancy Paulsen Press
  3. Oh The Places You’ll Go by Dr. Seuss/Harper Collins
  4. The Nameless God by Savie Karnel/RedPanda
  5. My Name is Gulab by Sagar Kolwankar/Tulika Books
  6. The Boy Who Asked Why by Swomya Rajendran/SatwikGade/Tulika Books
  7. Why Are You Afraid to Hold My Hand? By Sheila Dhir/Tulika Books
  8. Guthli Has Wings by Kanak Shashi/Tulika Books
  9. Loki Takes Guard by Menaka Raman/Talking Cub
  10. Rain Before Rainbow by Smriti Halls/David Litchfield/Walker Books
  11. The World Made A Rainbow by Michelle Robinson/Emily Hamilton/Bloomsbury Childrens Books
  12. Go Wild edited by Bijal Vachharajani, Various authors/Puffin
  13. We Hope: Children on Climate Change Various authors/Pratham
  14. Art is a Voice by Kripa/Art1ist
  15. Dear Mrs. Naidu by Mathangi Subramanium/Zubaan
  16. Change Sings by Amanda Gorman/Loren Lang/Puffin
  17. Miracle on Sunderbaag Street by Nandita Da Cunha/Priya Kuriyan/Kalpavriksh
  18. Miracle on Kacchua Beach by Nandita Da Cunha/Priya Kuriyan/Kalpavriksh
  19. Rock by Rock – The Fantastical Garden of Nek Chand by Jennifer Bradbury/Sam Boughton/Atheneum
  20. Behind the Lie by Asha Nehemiah/Aindri C./Pratham Books
  21. After the Fall by Dan Santat/Roaring Brook Press
  22. Roll of Thunder, Hear My Call by Mildred D. Taylor/Puffin
  23. When Stars are Scattered by Omar Mohamed and Victoria Jamieson/Faber and Faber
  24. Migrants by Issa Watanabe/Gecko Press
  25. Songs of Freedom Series-Tales of the Freedom Movement From All Over India by Various authors/ Duckbill
  26. Mukund and Riaz by Nina Sabnani/Tulika Books
  27. Roop and the River Crossing by Samina Mishra/Shivam Choudhari/Puffin. The Book of Hopes edited by Katherine Rundell/ Bloomsbury Childrens Books

JoAnne Saldanha, a.k.a "Myth Aunty", is a library educator and curator, who has been flying on the wings of tales for over 20 years as she navigates the world of children, books, and stories. She shares her work on www.mythaunty.com and @mythaunty.
item.excerpt:encoded:
JoAnne Saldanha
Books entertain, inspire, and even drive action. JoAnne Saldanha writes about how children’s literature can nurture resilience and help children believe in a better world.

item.title: The importance of savings and financial literacy from an early age
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Pratima Kishore Pai

I strongly believe in the saying "wealth builds wealth." With the right guidance, you can grow your wealth. Today’s youth live fast-paced lives. They want everything—expensive cars, houses, and all the best branded items at a young age. There’s nothing wrong with wanting to live well, but in their rush to own everything, many fall into debt.

Is buying a house really a good idea? Many would say yes, but if you think about it, property only increases in value significantly if it's an independent house. A flat might not appreciate as much over time. With the right knowledge, young people can better understand investments and how they work.

Social media often bring up reels which suggest that you can get rich quickly by making small investments. But in most cases, this is misleading, and it could be a trap to con people of their hard-earned money.

CBSE has taken a great step by introducing skill education for students from class 6. Among the skill subjects, financial literacy is offered, giving students the chance to learn about investments and stock trading. They even get to practice mock trading, which helps students understand how it all works.

If students learn about finances at an early age, they will be more careful when making investment decisions in the future. They’ll weigh the pros and cons and make smarter choices. Financial literacy helps them understand the power of compounding, allowing them to save more money.

Students who are financially literate from a young age develop responsible financial habits that stay with them throughout their lives. They will learn how to earn, spend, save, and invest wisely.

Financially literate people are also better at avoiding credit card debt, student loans, and other high-interest debt. Understanding borrowing and how to manage it will enable them to help and avoid the hassle of unnecessary debt.

Learning about basic investments like stocks, bonds, and mutual funds at an early age can lead to better opportunities for building wealth in the future, including investments in real estate and retirement savings. This will ensure that you can retire early if you want to and go globetrotting whenever you want to.

Managing finances early on gives people the confidence and skills to achieve financial independence. They will always walk that extra mile to ensure financial stability and possibly have no regrets. This means they can make their own decisions about money and won’t have to depend on others for support in adulthood.

Everyone faces financial ups and downs. Being financially literate helps people handle tough situations they may come across in life like losing a job or dealing with unexpected expenses. Knowing how to budget, save, and seek advice makes these challenges easier to handle.

Teaching kids good financial habits can have a lasting effect, not just on them but also on future generations. If children learn about saving, budgeting, and investing early, they can break cycles of financial struggle and create a culture of financial security. We can involve our kids in creating a monthly budget for household expenses so they understand that when money is limited, they should prioritize their needs based on necessity. Encouraging children to read newspapers like the Economic Times or financial magazines will help develop their knowledge and understanding of companies that attract investments, as well as how stocks rise and fall.

We should prioritize teaching our kids about finance. Opening a bank account for them allows them to store money and learn about interest rates and how the duration of an investment impacts its growth. They will also understand the importance of staying invested for the long term. It’s important to have discussions with your kids about banks and financial institutions, helping them learn about concepts like collateral and how loans work when there is no collateral. Such children may even research loan options available for entrepreneurs and the special benefits, particularly for female entrepreneurs. If more students pursue entrepreneurship, they will hire people as their businesses grow, which could help reduce poverty and unemployment in our country.

Understanding finances from a young age helps people set realistic goals, like saving for something big, investing for the future, or planning for retirement. People who are financially smart are more likely to reach their long-term goals because they manage their money carefully.

Pratima Kishore Pai is passionate about teaching and finds great joy in being around children, as their curiosity and enthusiasm inspires her to explore new ideas. She enjoys delving into various forms of art and embracing creative pursuits. She also has a keen interest in academic content development. Her siblings are her biggest cheerleaders, offering constant support in all her endeavours.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pratima Kishore Pai
Managing finances is a very important life skill and the sooner we introduce children to financial literacy, the better they will be at managing their finances.

item.title: Creating school libraries as safe spaces
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Akhila Arora

Schools play a very crucial role when it comes to shaping a child’s development. This development is not just academic, but also social and emotional. Ever since the concept of holistic wellbeing has started gaining recognition, it has become more and more important to develop school libraries as safe spaces. This article sheds light on the pivotal role of creating supportive as well as inclusive environments within schools, particularly libraries as a space for fostering open dialogue, acceptance, and understanding of mental health issues.

The concept of safe spaces

Safe spaces are defined as environments where individuals feel secure, valued, and respected, irrespective of their backgrounds or identities. The school library, traditionally a hub of knowledge, is evolving into a space that not only nurtures intellectual growth but also provides a safe haven for students dealing with the complexities of mental health.At the heart of any effective school library programme lies the importance of individual understanding of each child. In recent years, due to increasing usage of technology and it being introduced much earlier in a child’s life, the boundaries between real and virtual lives have blurred. This causes a lot of mental stress and has resulted in a rise in children dealing with mental health issues. This alone offers reason enough to think about how our school libraries are meeting the need for a comfortable, welcoming, personalized learning environment by providing a non-judgmental and distraction-free atmosphere where students can be their true selves without having to prove themselves to anyone.

Breaking the stigma

One of the primary challenges in addressing mental health issues is the pervasive stigma that surrounds them. By transforming school libraries into safe spaces, educators and administrators can actively contribute to destigmatizing mental health. The library becomes a platform for resources that promote understanding, empathy, and awareness, and fostering an atmosphere where students feel comfortable seeking help and discussing their struggles.

Inclusive literature

A key element in the transformation of school libraries into safe spaces is curating literature that reflects diverse perspectives on mental health. It has been an established fact that representation matters a lot and hence inclusive literature that includes narratives depicting characters dealing with various mental health challenges is vital for providing students with relatable stories and role models. By incorporating such material into the library collection, schools send a powerful message of acceptance and acknowledgment of mental health as an integral aspect of the human experience.

Professional guidance

Librarians, as information facilitators, play the central role when it comes to shaping the culture of the school library. Moreover, librarians can serve as allies in creating an atmosphere of trust, ensuring that students feel comfortable in approaching them for support.

Open dialogue and support groups

In addition to housing relevant literature, school libraries can host open dialogue sessions and support groups focused on mental health. These forums provide students with the opportunity to share their experiences, learn from each other, and engage in conversations that break down the barriers surrounding mental health all the while mentioning representative literature. Librarians and school counsellors can collaborate to facilitate these sessions, fostering a sense of community within the school.

Conclusion

Creating school libraries as safe spaces is an imperative step in creating inclusive and supportive environments within educational institutions. By actively addressing mental health through literature, professional guidance, open dialogue, educational programs, and innovative technologies, schools can contribute greatly to fighting misconceptions and breaking the stigma surrounding mental health issues. The transformation of libraries into hubs of mental health and awareness regarding its issues reflects a commitment to the overall wellbeing of students, giving birth to a culture of acceptance, understanding, and empathy that extends beyond the classroom. In doing so, schools become catalysts for positive change, shaping the future of education in a manner that prioritizes the holistic development of every student.

Some “feel good” books:

  1. "The Happiness Project" by Gretchen Rubin
    • Gretchen Rubin takes readers on a year-long journey to discover what makes us happy and provides actionable advice on how to incorporate positive habits into our lives.
  2. "Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy" by David D. Burns
    • Dr. Burns, a renowned psychiatrist, provides practical tools and techniques from cognitive-behavioural therapy to help individuals overcome depression and improve their mental wellbeing.
  3. "Mindset: The New Psychology of Success" by Carol S. Dweck
    • Carol Dweck explores the concept of mindset and how one's beliefs about their abilities can impact success, happiness, and overall wellbeing.
  4. "Man's Search for Meaning" by Viktor E. Frankl
    • Based on his experiences as a Holocaust survivor, Viktor Frankl explores the pursuit of meaning in life, emphasizing the importance of finding purpose in the face of adversity.
  5. "Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones" by James Clear
    • James Clear provides a practical guide to understanding habits, making positive changes, and building a foundation for long-term wellbeing.
  6. "The Gifts of Imperfection: Let Go of Who You Think You're Supposed to Be and Embrace Who You Are" by Brené Brown
    • Brené Brown explores the power of vulnerability, shame, resilience, and the importance of embracing imperfections for a more wholehearted life.

Akhila Arora is the Librarian at Bal Bharati Public School, Rohini. She can be reached at akhila.arora@rh.balbharati.org
item.excerpt:encoded:
Akhila Arora
Libraries are not just reading spaces anymore, they can and should be a lot more.

item.title: An autobiography of a novice teacher
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Abdul Basith

My schooling                                                    

As students, the knowledge we assimilate is shaped by our experiences and the teachers who nurture us. A teacher plays a pivotal role, much like a gardener tending to a garden, ensuring that each child blossoms with love, patience, and guidance. Reflecting on my journey, I feel grateful to all those educators who shared their knowledge with me and inspired me to become a teacher myself and help struggling students like me.

As a student, I went to different schools in Kerala. Malayalam is my mother tongue, and all other languages were new for me. From primary school to the seventh grade, I studied in a Malayalam-medium school. However, in eighth grade, I transitioned to an English-medium school. I struggled with English grammar and spellings. But, the unwavering support of my parents and the encouragement of a dedicated teacher made this transition smoother. My English teacher’s influence instilled in me a love for learning and a commitment to teaching.

Stepping in to the education field

My decision to pursue education as a career was shaped by an individual who left a profound impact on my life. After completing my BA in English Literature, I contemplated enrolling in a B.Ed. program. However, the fieldwork aspect of an MA in Education piqued my interest, as it would provide me with firsthand experience of real classroom dynamics. The teaching internship I did during this course helped me understand that teaching extends beyond textbooks—it involves navigating ground realities and encouraging meaningful learning experiences.

The joy in those students’ eyes who were formerly pushed aside for falling behind is what inspires me the most as a teacher. The smiles we bring on and the hope we inspire are the sparks that keep me on walking this path. I witnessed an incident where a teacher hesitated to acknowledge a student’s effort because of her preconceived notions. However, the class’s collective insistence ensured that the student was rewarded fairly, reinforcing my belief that judgments and prejudices have no place in a teacher’s heart. Every child deserves a fair chance.

My journey as a teacher

I strive to create an inclusive and supportive environment where students feel valued and are encouraged to make mistakes and learn from them. In my classroom, boys and girls sit together in pairs. I frequently rearrange seating for group activities, ensuring that every student receives attention and engagement. My students see me as a friend who values their contributions, irrespective of their skill levels.

In my third week of teaching, students asked, "Will you remember us when you leave? Will you invite us to your wedding?" These questions reflected the bond I had built with them. Their excitement and curiosity during interactive activities affirmed their preference for engaging lessons over rote learning. I strive to balance my subject expertise with an understanding of students' perspectives, learning levels, and cultural backgrounds.

Reflecting on my teaching journey, I have realized that empathy, resilience, and faith in every child's potential are crucial. My first teaching experience was filled with both frustration and inspiration. I recall the heartbreak of hearing a third-grade student express his fear of English due to the corporal punishment he had received in the past. This moment solidified my commitment to providing a safe, nurturing learning environment.

One particular moment remains etched in my memory. I once wrote a Hindi word incorrectly on the board, and my students corrected me. This instance reinforced my belief that teaching is a reciprocal process of learning and growth. Small victories, such as a previously struggling student confidently narrating a story, made my efforts worthwhile. The spontaneous applause from his peers validated the inclusive environment I aimed to create.

I believe that all children can learn. This principle guides my lesson planning, ensuring inclusivity by accommodating diverse learning styles and abilities. Recognizing each child's unique strengths helps me encourage them to reach their full potential.

Building relationships with students has been one of the most fulfilling aspects of my teaching journey. Their enthusiastic greetings, engagement in lessons, and eagerness to share their thoughts highlight the relational foundation of teaching. On Children’s Day, their excitement to wear colourful clothes and participate in activities demonstrated the power of simple acknowledgments in creating a positive school culture. The depth of our bond was most evident on the last day of my teaching internship when students presented heartfelt gifts—pens, flowers, and handwritten notes. A girl tightly held my hand, her tear-filled eyes whispering, "Please don’t forget us." Her words affirmed the impact I had made, making every challenge and effort worthwhile.

Inspired by Palmer’s philosophy of "teaching beyond technique" and recognizing "the teacher within," (which he talks about in The Courage to Teach: Exploring the Inner Landscape of a Teacher’s Life) I realized that effective teaching is not solely about methods but about forming genuine connections with students. Teaching demands emotional investment and self-reflection, and the bonds I built with my students proved that education is fundamentally about relationships.

Starting my teaching career from scratch presented numerous challenges. The transition from being a student to standing in front of a classroom offered new insights into the realities of teaching. One striking experience was seeing a usually engaged student suddenly fall silent, her eyes brimming with tears. Upon inquiry, she shared that a lesson had triggered memories of her late father. At that moment, I realized that teachers must navigate not only academic learning but also students’ emotions and histories with empathy and care.

My journey forced me to confront and challenge my own biases. In the beginning, I observed that boys and girls hesitated to sit together in group activities. Initially, I viewed this as resistance, but deeper reflection revealed that cultural norms shaped this behaviour. Instead of imposing change, I gradually encouraged collaboration. This experience underscored the significance of cultural sensitivity in education.

As I reflect on my teaching journey, I realize that my smile has been my constant companion. My smile of warmth each morning while being greeted by my

students, my smile of strength in the class when my students understand what I am teaching, my smile of assurance when that student who was ignored by everyone raises his hand for answering a question, my smile of acceptance in difficult situations, and my smile of positivity when I see my students effectively using the TLMs (Teaching Learning Materials) I made. Just as my students' smiles lit up the classroom, mine lit up my heart. I have learned that teaching is not just about giving knowledge, but about sharing joy, empathy, and resilience. As I close this chapter, I smile with the hope that the lessons I have taught and the love I have shared will continue to shape the lives of my students.

Abdul Basith is a BA English Literature Graduate, now pursuing MA Education in Azim Premji University, Bangalore. He can be reached at abdul.basithp23_mae@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Abdul Basith
A young student-teacher shares the lessons he learnt while working with students during a short teaching internship stint that he did as part of his MA Education course.

item.title: “Distribution of power” in democracies
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: Last time you quoted Madhav Khosla as saying that the Constitution is also meant to serve as a pedagogical tool to educate us into a democratic culture. But we know so little of the Constitution – rights and duties – and even lesser about how the Constitution functions in governmental spaces. As citizens, we know our rights and duties, which is such a small part of the Constitution – barely 30 pages of the total 260 and more pages. There must be a lot more to the Constitution that we need to be aware of.

I vaguely remember learning about the Legislature, Executive, and Judiciary and how they ought to be separated from each other. I don’t quite get what the purpose of it all was, and in what way it is mandated in the Constitution. I remember them as the three pillars of our democracy, but how and why are they different and separated?

Anubhuti: Yes, it is quite complex. It is fairly clear what the Judiciary is – courts and judges who uphold the Constitution, and the laws derived from them like the Indian Penal Code (now Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita), family law, civil law, etc. The Judiciary ensures that laws are being interpreted correctly and are being applied in the right situation in the right way. It also recommends new laws from its experience in specific cases and points out flaws in any law recommended by the Legislature.

Legislatures are the representatives of all people – the entire parliament – and they have the authority to make laws and recommend amendments to the Constitution since they represent the general will. We need to remember that the Legislature includes the opposition parties as well.

The Executive is separate from the Legislature. The Executive includes the government, which is the party or parties in power, the president, the extensive bureaucracy, and the police system who implement the laws.

Ira: Hmmm… I get that. But why worry about separation of powers? They should work together in harmony, shouldn’t they? I think we human beings unnecessarily politicize everything.

Surely, whether they are Legislature or Executive, they do not have power over us citizens. Their power is limited to the existing laws, which have been decided upon and accepted by us citizens.

Anubhuti: Yes, you are right in that their power over us is limited to the laws that have been ratified by the Judiciary. But the Legislature also has the power to come up with new laws, or to change existing laws. They are internally regulated by the opposition, by the need for a majority for significant laws, and they are also regulated by the president (the head of the state) who ratifies laws.

In India, the Judiciary can also point out if a new law, or modification or repeal of an existing law abides by the Constitution. If this upsets any fundamental principle of the Constitution, the Judiciary can nullify a law. The Judiciary is also in a way regulated by the Executive. Appointment of judges is recommended and approved by the president.

Ira: What is the role of the bureaucracy in all this?

Anubhuti: Well, the Executive represents people’s general will, but they are not legal experts or for that matter experts in any other policy area, whether industrialization, environmental issues, finance, political science, sociology, etc. The bureaucracy, therefore, helps convert the general will into specific policies and laws with their knowledge.

Ira: So the buck stops at the Executive and bureaucratic machinery! That makes sense. We need experts to manage the country, not just politicians.

Anubhuti: Ha ha ha. Do you realize the serious problem with what you are saying? You seem to be proposing epistocracy – ruling by knowledge and expertise. That argument is a slippery slope. You want experts to rule the country, then you will have to say we have to listen to everything they say, and then any law they come up with has to be accepted by us. Our life will be controlled by them. We will have to live the way they decide we should live. We, who are not experts in anything, will not have a say in law or policy. In principle that is contradictory to democracy, which is basically rule of the demos, the people.

Ira: What! Yes, you are right. Experts know their subject areas, but how can they know how others should live their lives? I would not want a scientist, a technocrat, a historian, political scientist, or even a philosopher to tell me how I should live my life. Isn’t that the basic principle of democracy – everyone has their own idea of a good life, and the state should not be deciding how we live our lives?

Anubhuti: Exactly! The Right to Freedom is not only speech, expression, practising the religion we want. All of them put together amount to the Right to Life – living the way we want to live.

Therefore, the Executive is also controlled or regulated by the Judiciary. They act as a balance to decrease the power the Executive (let me say government) has over people. Again, the basis on which the Judiciary makes this judgment is the Constitution. The Judiciary is also regulated by constitutional mandates.

Ira: So this is a triangular relationship. Legislature is regulated by the Judiciary, and they in turn regulate the Executive. The Executive is regulated by the Judiciary and the Legislature (through the opposition). Does that make the Legislature – representatives of the people – most powerful?

Anubhuti: The triangle does not end there. The Legislature is also internally regulated by the opposition, and the laws and policies they enact have to be ratified by the Judiciary. That completes the triangle – if you can imagine the overlap in their functions as the vertices of the triangle.

Ira: How is all this mandated by the Constitution?

Anubhuti: In two ways. One, by clearly identifying the functions of each pillar of our democratic state. Two, the overlaps in their functions and jurisdictions are also clearly articulated in the Constitution. Why do you think it is considered one of the largest constitutions in the world?

Ira: Tell me the fundamental reason why such a complex situation has been created?

Anubhuti: It all goes back to a French philosopher and judge called Baron de Montesquie. He famously said that power in any state should not reside in one arm of the state – Legislature, Executive, or Judiciary, because that will then result in tyranny. For instance, if all the power resided in the Legislature, it is the tyranny of the majority who voted for the incumbent government.

Ira: Aah! Now I see where the phrase tyranny of the majority comes from.

Anubhuti: I find Deendayal Upadhyay’s statements very useful here. He said, “The Legislature, the Judiciary or the people, none of these is supreme. Some will say ‘Why! People are sovereign. They elect.’ But even the people are not sovereign, because people too, have no right to act against Dharma. If an elected government allows people to go against Dharma and does not punish them, then that government is in reality a government of thieves….The Legislature, the Judiciary or the people, none of these is supreme.” When I read this, I replaced Dharma with the basic structure of the Constitution and found myself agreeing wholeheartedly with him!

Ira: Come to think of it, once we vote and a government is formed, we have to let them, and this triangle of pillars of democracy, rule us – at least for five years.

Anubhuti: Aren’t you forgetting something? What about the fourth pillar? Media and civil society. Ideally, the media is supposed to be neutral and oversee the government and even the Judiciary. They make choices of who to invite in popular debates to critically analyze all significant decisions made by the State (a combination of the aforementioned three pillars). Not all experts are part of the bureaucracy, isn’t it? Many of them raise their voices against any decision when they see mistakes. The primary role of civil society is this – just in case they also find the popular media biased.

Ira: Not a triangle, you are describing a pentagon – Legislature, Executive, Judiciary, Media, and Civil Society. Not to mention active citizenship which most of us practise or are expected to. We have the right to ask for information from the government, raise questions, or even submit petitions challenging all these pillars.

Anubhuti: Democracies are so complex and beautiful. The world would be boring and mundane, or even tyrannical, if not for these complexities.

Ira: I am reminded of this joke I heard. Democracy may not be the perfect solution to organize societies, but this is the best solution human beings have come up with…so far!

Anubhuti: Yes, and democracy has so many variants. Nowadays, the political discourse is about Republicanism – direct democracy practised by all citizens. Let’s see how that works in India.

You know democracy ought to be dynamic and ever-changing. If we become static and complacent, we are not being democratic. It is a lot of continuous work. Democracy is eternally a work-in-progress – always in the becoming, but never being.

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Legislature, Executive, Judiciary – do they rule us, or do we rule them? Prakash Iyer continues the thought-provoking dialogue about how democracy is an ever-evolving system of accountability and balance.
