item.title: How a school changed the life of a student: Akankshi’s journey
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ravindra Rawat

Education is not merely about imparting academic knowledge, it is about nurturing individuals to become confident, independent members of the society. Schools play a vital role in shaping the lives of students, especially those facing unique challenges. This is the story of Akankshi, a student who entered Government Junior High School, Masoli in 2022 with significant emotional and behavioural difficulties. With patience, empathy, and use of innovative educational practices, Akankshi’s journey is a testament to how a school can profoundly transform a student’s life.

Background

When Akankshi joined our school in the 6th grade, she carried an overwhelming set of challenges. As teachers, Bikram Singh Bhandari (Headmaster In-charge), Bharat Singh Negi, and I (Ravindra Rawat) were confronted with a student who was isolated, fearful, and withdrawn. Akankshi barely spoke, except to her neighbour and classmate Manashi. Her distress was palpable – she always entered the classroom after everyone else and whenever someone closed the door to the classroom, she would break into loud, uncontrollable sobs. Her anxiety was so severe that she was unable to go to the washroom alone and would frequently wet her clothes. Her situation seemed hopeless, and expecting her to read or write even a single word seemed impossible.

We began to observe her closely and realized that Akankshi might be dealing with both psychological trauma and intellectual disability. To help her, we focused on understanding her emotional needs while providing her with support and love.

Understanding the roots of her anxiety

In order to better address Akankshi’s challenges, we reached out to her family and gathered insights from her friends and previous school. We learnt that Akankshi’s parents had to leave her bedroom door open at night until she fell asleep, as closing the door would lead to tears and distress. Even at home, she could only use the washroom with the door open and her mother standing nearby. An incident from her childhood appeared to be a key factor in her behaviour – Akankshi had once been accidentally locked in a washroom, which left her with a deep fear of closed doors.

However, her fear extended beyond this traumatic event. She spoke very little, had few friends, and seemed disconnected from her environment. We concluded* that Akankshi was struggling with an anxiety disorder, likely a combination of claustrophobia (fear of closed spaces) and selective mutism (an inability to speak in certain social situations). While we couldn’t fully diagnose her level of cognitive ability, we knew our first priority was to address her emotional wellbeing before focusing on her academic progress.

Our goal for Akankshi

We wanted to help Akankshi become a confident and independent individual who could function in the society. To achieve this, we devised a two-phase approach:

Phase 1: Addressing psychological challenges and building interest in learning

  1. Build emotional security and trust

Our first step was to create a supportive environment where Akankshi felt safe and understood. Establishing trust between her, her teachers, and her classmates was crucial. We consistently showed her empathy and encouraged positive interactions.

2. Overcome anxiety and fear of closed spaces

Given her fear of closed doors, we gently exposed her to situations that required her to confront this anxiety, such as using the toilet. We provided constant support, encouraging her to gradually face her fears and build independence.

3. Enhance social interaction and communication

We involved her in group activities, particularly those that promoted social interaction. We also encouraged her to engage in verbal communication, aiming to reduce her selective mutism over time.

4. Improve self-confidence

Activities like dance and yoga were introduced to help her express herself physically and emotionally. These activities not only boosted her self-esteem but also provided a creative outlet for her emotions.

Phase 2: Developing academic skills and independence

  1. Promote academic engagement

Once she felt secure, we introduced basic academic tasks. We began with simple class 2-level Hindi reading and writing, as well as counting and basic arithmetic. The focus was on effort rather than accuracy, allowing her to make mistakes without the fear of criticism.

2. Develop independence

We encouraged Akankshi to take on small daily tasks independently, such as using the toilet alone. Our goal was to prepare her for greater autonomy and personal growth.

Behavioural intervention strategies

The transformation in Akankshi’s behaviour was made possible through a series of carefully planned interventions:

  1. Counselling her mother and classmates

We counselled both her mother and classmates to ensure that everyone around Akankshi understood her needs. This created a supportive environment, both at home and at school.

2. Building confidence through activities

By involving her in non-verbal activities such as dance and yoga, we helped Akankshi develop her self-confidence. As she excelled in these activities, she became more willing to participate in other aspects of school life, such as doing her homework, cooperating with classmates, gardening, etc.

3. Positive reinforcement

Every small success was celebrated. By praising her for even the tiniest accomplishment, we built her self-esteem and encouraged her to keep trying.

4. Daily positive interaction

Simple gestures like a daily smile from her teachers helped create a welcoming and safe environment, making her feel more comfortable and accepted.

5. Group support for toilet usage

A group of supportive classmates accompanied her to the toilet, reducing her fear of closed doors and gradually helped her build independence.

6. Unconditional communication

We spoke to Akankshi regularly, even when she didn’t respond. This continuous communication reassured her that her teachers were there for her, creating a safe space for her to eventually open up.

7. Participation in leading school prayers

Akankshi was given the responsibility to lead the school in prayers, which helped her build confidence in public speaking and contributed to her emotional growth.

8. Encouraging a positive self-image

We constantly reinforced that she was brave, loved, and capable of overcoming her fears. This helped her reshape her internal self-image and boosted her emotional resilience.

9. Flexible approach to learning

Initially, we didn’t pressurize her to engage in academic tasks. Once she was ready, we introduced writing tasks, allowing her to make mistakes without the fear of criticism. This approach helped her develop a positive attitude towards learning.

Results

After two years of consistent support, Akankshi has made significant progress:

  1. Increased independence

She now uses the toilet independently and locks the door without fear.

2. Overcoming claustrophobia

She no longer cries when doors are closed and sleeps in her room with the door closed.

3. Emotional security

Akankshi feels secure in school and has developed strong relationships with both teachers and classmates.

4. Active participation

She participates in various school activities and engages with her peers, demonstrating improved social skills.

5. Improved communication

She now speaks to other students and occasionally responds verbally to teachers, a major breakthrough in her communication skills.

6. Happiness and wellbeing

Akankshi is visibly happier at school, and her sense of wellbeing has greatly improved.

7. Academic engagement

Although her writing contains many errors, she is keen to learn. She attempts to complete homework and fills her notebooks with effort, showing a strong interest in learning.

Conclusion

While Akankshi has made tremendous progress, she still requires specialized support to continue her development. The teachers who worked with her were not counsellors or educators for differently-abled children, yet they did everything within their capacity to support her. After the transfer of the headmaster and the other two teachers, her new teachers are now focusing on her academics. Now that she is ready to learn, it is crucial that her new teachers continue to show patience and understanding. Her journey has shown that with time, dedication, and care, significant transformation is possible. The phrase “Hurry spoils the curry” aptly applies to Akankshi – her success will come with patience and perseverance.

*The author’s conclusions were based on a three month course from IGNOU called Foundation Course on Education of Children with Disabilities (FCED) and on his extensive research of Akankshi’s symptoms on the internet.

Ravindra Rawat is an experienced upper primary teacher with 26 years of teaching experience, including seven years as a BRC and CRC coordinator under Sarva Siksha Abhiyan, providing academic support to teachers and schools. He has expertise in e-content creation and has served as Master Trainer in various teacher training programs. He has also published two ebooks. He can be reached at ravi.mrawat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ravindra Rawat
Education is much more than just textbooks and exams. Ravindra Rawat writes about how a school brought about a student’s life-changing transformation from fear to confidence.

item.title: Navigating morality in the digital age
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

Social media has completely changed the way we talk about morality. It’s made ethical conversations more immediate, more public, and sometimes more intense than ever before. On the one hand, this has sparked real change – exposing injustices, mobilizing activism, and holding people accountable. On the other, it’s also led to misinformation, division, and a culture where moral outrage can sometimes overshadow thoughtful discussion.

Think about the #MeToo movement in India. It started with a few brave voices speaking up, but quickly gained momentum as more survivors came forward. Thanks to social media, they could bypass traditional gatekeepers and demand accountability from powerful figures. The result? Resignations, policy changes, and a broader cultural shift in how workplace harassment is viewed. Similar things happened in Myanmar during the 2021 protests against the military coup. With state-controlled media suppressing dissent, platforms like Twitter (now X) and Facebook became essential tools for activists to organize and share real-time updates with the world.

But there’s another side to this rapid spread of information. When issues go viral, the pressure to react immediately can lead to rushed decisions. Public outrage – often justified – sometimes escalates to what feels like digital mob justice. A single tweet, an old post taken out of context, or a mistake can quickly turn into a full-blown cancellation. The problem with this? It leaves little room for people to learn, apologize, or grow. The fear of being “cancelled” can make people hesitant to share honest thoughts, stifling open discussion instead of encouraging it.

This performative aspect of morality is another tricky area. In a world where online approval is currency, it’s easy to fall into the trap of posting about social issues just to be seen as virtuous. Trending hashtags and viral posts can bring attention to causes, but without real action behind them, they risk becoming empty gestures. Social media can amplify voices, but true activism needs to extend beyond the screen. As Martin Luther King Jr. once said, "Our lives begin to end the day we become silent about things that matter." Speaking up is crucial, but meaningful action must follow.

Human nature also plays a big role in all of this. People crave belonging, and online spaces amplify this instinct. Sometimes, this is a good thing – helping movements like climate activism gain traction. But it can also mean that people go along with whatever is popular in their digital circles, even if they have doubts. History and psychology tell us that people often conform to group opinions, even when they personally disagree. That’s why it’s so important to have spaces where people can express different viewpoints without the fear of being attacked.

Then there’s the issue of moral disengagement – the way social media sometimes makes us forget that there are real people behind the screens. The anonymity of the internet can embolden cruelty, and the consequences can be devastating. One heartbreaking example is a 16-year-old Malaysian girl who took her own life after running an Instagram poll asking followers if she should live or die. The fact that some people actually encouraged her to end her life is a chilling reminder of how online culture can be dehumanizing.

This dehumanization happens on a larger scale too. Misinformation spreads rapidly, and it’s not just about fake news – it’s about eroding trust in reliable sources. Political campaigns have been manipulated, elections influenced, and entire populations misled by online propaganda. And because algorithms push content based on what we already engage with, we end up stuck in “filter bubbles,” seeing only opinions that reinforce our existing beliefs. This deepens divisions, making it harder to consider alternative perspectives.

Public shaming is another ethical minefield. Holding people accountable is necessary, but social media often turns judgment into a spectacle. The problem isn’t just that people are being criticized – it’s that the outrage spreads so quickly and intensely that it can ruin lives before all the facts are clear. Platforms like X (formerly teacher), in particular, have become digital courtrooms where verdicts are passed instantly, sometimes without context or nuance.

Cyberbullying is one of the most serious moral issues online. The tragic case of K-pop star Sulli, who took her own life after relentless online harassment, sparked much-needed conversations about mental health and digital responsibility. Online abuse disproportionately affects women, LGBTQ+ individuals, and marginalized communities, making it clear that social media platforms need better policies and accountability measures. As author Brené Brown puts it, "Shame corrodes the very part of us that believes we are capable of change." Instead of shaming, we should focus on fostering understanding and growth.

So, where do we go from here? First, we need to be more critical consumers of content. Before sharing something, it’s worth asking: Is this true? Who benefits from my believing this? Second, digital literacy should be a priority. Some countries, like Singapore and India, have already started integrating media literacy into education, but this needs to happen on a larger scale. People of all ages need tools to navigate online spaces responsibly, recognize bias, and think critically about the information they consume.

Social media companies also have a major role to play. Stronger policies against misinformation, hate speech, and harassment can make platforms safer and more ethical. Governments, too, are starting to crack down on fake news, but regulation alone isn’t enough. The real change needs to come from within these tech companies – rethinking business models that thrive on outrage and engagement at the expense of truth and wellbeing.

At the end of the day, social media is just a reflection of who we are. It magnifies both the best and worst aspects of human behaviour. The key is balance – using these platforms to drive positive change while being aware of their pitfalls. Technology doesn’t control us; we control it. If we engage thoughtfully, stay informed, and foster respectful dialogue, we can ensure that social media is a tool for progress rather than harm. Ethical engagement in the digital age is a shared responsibility, requiring effort from individuals, educators, policymakers, and tech companies alike. Only by working together can we create an online world that aligns with our highest values.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A GenZ in spirit, with the benefit of hindsight, she uses her 37 years in media and education to help leverage generational diversity at work and at home. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
Social media has amplified ethical conversations, driving activism but also enabling misinformation and online shaming. Neerja Singh explores how we can navigate this digital moral landscape responsibly.

item.title: From classrooms to real life: math education for practical thinking
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sweety Rastogi

The ultimate goal of education is to empower individuals with the skills necessary to navigate and succeed in the complexities of everyday life. Unfortunately, the current educational system often prioritizes performance in exams over the development of these essential life skills.

In our current education system, both teachers and students are trapped in a relentless cycle of pressure. Teachers are burdened with the demands of completing the syllabus, conducting rigorous revisions, and achieving high results in board examinations, often measured by the number of students scoring a perfect 100. This pressure incentivizes teachers to prioritize exam-oriented teaching strategies, potentially neglecting the development of critical thinking, creativity, and real-world application of knowledge.

Students are primarily focused on achieving high scores to secure admission to prestigious institutions. This leads them to prioritize rote learning, memorization techniques, and shortcuts, rather than developing a deep understanding of concepts.

While this 'rat race' can end for students upon achieving their desired academic goals, it continues unabated for teachers, creating a constant cycle of stress and a potential neglect of their true role as educators.

Incident 1: While at the vegetable market purchasing my weekly fruits and vegetables, I inquired about the price of apples. A young boy, approximately 10 years old, swiftly responded, "They're 150 Rs per kg." When I asked for the final amount, he said that the apples were 1.75 kg, so I had to pay Rs 262.50. Impressed, I couldn't resist asking if he attended school. He replied, "I need to survive, so I can't afford to make mistakes with money." On my way home, I found myself pondering how the young boy at the market might have performed such swift mental calculations. The following day, as a warm-up exercise in my class of 10th graders, I challenged my students to calculate the actual price of the apples without using pen and paper.

The majority of the students were not able to complete the task due to their reliance on pen and paper. Only two students came close to the answer, but even they couldn't achieve exact accuracy. It's ironic that necessity honed the mental calculation skills of someone who did not attend school, while my students struggled for quick mental responses.

As a teacher, I see it as my failure for not instilling that necessity in my students. Are we truly acknowledging and valuing quick mental responses? If not, why is that the case?

The easiest way of solving the above problem mentally is not by the unitary method, which most students were using but the following

Price of apples weighing 1.75 kg = Rs (150+75+37.50) = Rs 262.50

Math textbooks and teaching methods typically lack interconnections between topics, leaving students unprepared to make these links when encountering them for the first time.

Teaching often follows a chapter-by-chapter approach, with assessments mirroring this structure. For instance, relating the unitary method with percentages could simplify calculations, yet this integration is often lacking. Instead, blueprints are typically structured around chapters rather than overarching concepts.

Incident 2: During a math exam, a student caught the attention of the invigilator by doing something peculiar with his question paper that no one else was doing. The invigilator, visibly annoyed, approached the student and discovered that he had torn out a piece of paper with a triangle on it. Accusing the student of attempting to cheat, the invigilator sternly warned him.

In response, the student innocently explained, "I'm checking whether the triangles are congruent or not because it's not specified in the question paper that they're not to scale. So, I'm allowed to scale and compare them by overlapping."

As educators, we craft assessments or design question papers in a manner that provides students with a clear understanding of the types of questions they can expect. Consequently, during examinations, approximately 90% of the students typically overlook the necessity to thoroughly read each question.

Moreover, we hardly find a question with the image ‘to scale’. But why aren’t we asking such questions?

Crafting effective math questions involves understanding learning objectives and designing diverse, clear, and contextually relevant questions that promote critical thinking and assess various skill levels.

Incident 3: During my visit to my cousin's house, her 10 year-old daughter informed me about her upcoming math periodic test on multiplication. When I asked her why she wasn't studying for the exam, she confidently responded that she was ready because she knew the test would involve multiplication, whether in word problems or other formats.

Can we infuse uniqueness into the topic of multiplication and integrate it with other concepts? By intertwining these concepts, we create a dynamic learning experience that evolves with each iteration. See the different types of questions intertwined with multiplication that even a 15 year old would want to try.

Can we design assessment questions that intrigue and engage students, encourage diverse approaches to problem-solving and offer opportunities for students to discover new insights and deepen their understanding?

It is time to re-imagine mathematics education. By moving beyond traditional teaching methods and embracing innovative approaches that integrate concepts, encourage exploration, and foster a love of learning, we can unlock the true potential of mathematics for all students.

By transforming mathematics from a subject feared by many to a tool for exploration and discovery, we can empower students to become confident problem-solvers and active participants in shaping the future.

Sweety Rastogi is a teaching practitioner who has been advocating for liberating the education process to accommodate change. She considers teachers as catalysts of change in transforming the education process. She calls herself a co-learner in the journey she is undertaking with her students. Sweety has 26 years of experience in the field of education as a teacher, mentor, curriculum planner and moderator. She is presently working as HOD (Math) in Learning Paths School, Mohali. She can be reached at srastogitms@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sweety Rastogi
Can math be about real-life problem-solving? Sweety Rastogi writes about how educators can make math more intuitive and practical for students.

item.title: Dealing and healing in the classroom
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Megha Bajaj

Recently, a young boy jumped off the terrace in a neighbouring building because he scored fewer marks than expected in his board exams. I did not know the boy, but this incident left me feeling miserable for several days. So, here I am, trying to make sense of things, and hopefully along the way, I will also be able to offer insights to create a more meaningful classroom environment.

Failure is something that all of us, including children, have faced or will face. Celebrating achievements is easy for children, but dealing with failure can be challenging. And in a typical classroom, failing is one of the most embarrassing life situations. Hence, it needs more attention.

As an educator and from my interactions with young people, I came to realize three important things about failure:

  • The first, most children fear failure to an extent that they prefer to give up even before they begin.
  • The second, children think of failure as the final end to all their dreams and goals! They don’t see it as a process but as an end in itself, and hence it hits them hard.
  • The third, for children thoughts about what others will think or feel about their failure matters more than the failure itself.

Children are highly dependent on adult responses while dealing with difficult emotions, and how we react makes a huge impact on the way children understand failure. Processing difficult life situations becomes easy for children if they are in a nurturing environment. They keenly observe the reactions of parents and other stakeholders when they fail and base their conclusions on what they observe. Failure could be big or small. It could be difficulty in completing or performing a task, difficulty in building blocks or colouring a picture, or even relationship issues with peers or families. Children’s responses to our reaction differ by age, intensity, and frequency. Some children may let things go easily and some could dwell on them for longer. So, much of how children react depends on how YOU as an educator or parent respond to a child’s failure. Make it a big deal, and they become unforgiving to themselves. Treat failure with compassion and they understand that failure is just a part of life.

If you are a teacher (or stakeholder), here are five things you can do to make every child accept, understand, and celebrate failure as part of life’s journey.

1. Show some love! –Failure is hard to understand. The first thing to offer is support. An assurance that the world hasn't changed, parents and people in the family have not changed, the attachment remains and you are still open to offer love. It is essential to show acceptance, support, and comfort through words and gestures.

For example: If a child receives low marks in a test, let the child know that you are happy to help and that you are keen to see the child grow and try again. Let them know your affections haven't changed because of the marks. This helps them accept failure more easily.

2. Build persistence – Persistence is what drives actions, Irrespective of whether a child ‘wins’ or not, encourage them to complete the task at hand. These encouragements indicate that efforts are important, that completing a task with dedication is vital and is what matters. This could help children view success differently; it’s not always about being first in a race, but running to the best of their ability.

3. I do, you do, we do –Teaching is less preaching and more showing. Children have to observe in order to understand better. Rather than over instructing, if teachers show how to deal with difficult emotions, children are likely to learn from their behaviours. I do, you do, and we do is an evidence-based approach to encourage healthy habits and behaviours.

For example: After failure in constructing blocks or doing a project, a teacher could say “Let’s try another way,” or “Let me think of a new way to solve this,” or “Let me try it this way,” or “I will try harder than give up!” These could be powerful statements which could act as tools to deal with failure. It also helps when teachers talk of their own failures to let children know that no one is perfect and failure is just a step towards success.

4. Praise efforts – Talk to your children about efforts rather than the results. Teachers could appreciate children for specific behaviours or simple efforts rather than discuss the end results.

For example: “I loved the way you didn't give up, despite struggling to balance.” Or, “I loved the way you continued and completed the task,” or “I was so happy to see you working hard on that project.” The intent here is to talk about behaviours rather than target the child.

Appreciating specific efforts could help children understand that the process is important and meaningful. It could also help them perceive failure in better ways. It is vital to understand that stakeholders cannot protect children from failing, but showing them that it is natural and they can simply try again. A new study by French researchers found that children who were told learning can be difficult, and that failing is a natural part of the learning process, actually performed better on tests than kids who were not given such reassurances.

5. Provide opportunities – The tricky part is to encourage children again, after an incidence of failure. It is extremely important that teachers and stakeholders don’t force children to try again immediately and avoid being pushy. It is advisable to be mindful of the child’s behaviours and give time, space, support, and comfort than critically analyze their performances. Encourage the child to share their concerns and resistances. Encouraging words and a nurturing environment can help children perform better. Every child could processes things differently. Our job is to help them through the hard times and provide tools, demonstrate behaviours, and model coping skills.

For example: When you see a child dejected about something, give him some time to cope with it rather than push him to try again, which may result in another failure. Communicating, helping them understand where the problem lies, and assurance can go a long way in getting children to get up again after a fall.

It wouldn't be wise to protect children from failure, it would also not be a good idea to preach that achievements don't matter, but what is important to imbibe is that everyone has the ability to rise again, to begin again, and to learn again!

Let’s create an education system, where a child is made life-ready and not just exam-ready.

Megha Bajaj is a bestselling author of several books, including, The Breakthrough published by Rupa Publications. She is a TEDx Speaker, an author-mentor, a passionate edupreneur, and an ardent seeker. She was among the 50 Most Influential Authors mentioned by Delhi Wire and 25 Most Inspiring Women in Mumbai. Her company WoW (Wonders of Words) conducts notable programs like the WoW Online Writing and Healing Workshop as well as WoW 3Ls (Language + Life + Leadership Skills) Program which has impacted 1,50,000 students and 10,000 teachers over 6 cities so far. She can be reached at info@wondersofwords.org or know more about her at www.MeghaBajajWoW.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Megha Bajaj
Children can feel overwhelmed by failure. Megha Bajaj shares five ways for educators and parents to respond and help children grow.

item.title: A celebration of science and discovery: IISc Bengaluru Open Day 2025
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anuradha C

The Indian Institute of Science (IISc) in Bengaluru hosted its annual Open Day on March 1, 2025, transforming its sprawling campus into a vibrant hub of scientific exploration and discovery. This event, held from 9 am to 5 pm, offered the public a unique opportunity to delve into the world of cutting-edge research and innovation.

A spectrum of scientific showcases

The Open Day featured a diverse array of activities designed to engage and inspire visitors of all ages. Departments and centres across the campus organized popular lectures, experimental demonstrations, poster presentations, quiz contests, scientific competitions, and exhibitions. These events provided attendees with a comprehensive view of the institute's advancements in science and technology.

Special activities were tailored for young students, aiming to ignite their interest in science and technology. Hands-on demonstrations allowed children to interact directly with scientific phenomena, fostering a deeper understanding and appreciation for the subjects.

Departments at the forefront

Several departments stood out with their engaging presentations:

  • Department of Aerospace Engineering offered simulations and models of aircraft and spacecraft, allowing visitors to grasp the complexities of aerodynamics and design.
  • Centre for Neuroscience displayed interactive exhibits on brain function, providing insights into neural networks and cognitive processes.
  • Department of Materials Engineering showcased innovations in nanotechnology and material sciences, highlighting their applications in various industries.
  • Department of Computer Science and Automation presented the latest in artificial intelligence and machine learning, including real-time demonstrations of AI applications.
  • Department of Civil Engineering featured a fascinating exhibit on crowd control risk models, using real-time sensor data. This system which had collected data during the Maha Kumbh Mela, demonstrated how data analytics can help prevent stampedes and manage large gatherings efficiently henceforth.
  • Centre for Water Sciences demonstrated a ground water survey and forecasting model that utilizes satellite and sensor data. Visitors learned how this technology helps predict water availability and manage resources sustainably.
  • Department of Mechanical Engineering displayed the quirky Cibycle, a bicycle that turns left when the handlebars are turned right, and vice versa. This counterintuitive engineering experiment had visitors attempting (and failing) to ride it, emphasizing the importance of muscle memory and adaptability in mechanical control systems.
  • Department of Materials Science allowed visitors to play with an intriguing non-Newtonian fluid – a type of "play dough" that felt gooey when a hand was gently immersed but turned rock solid when punched. This demonstration beautifully illustrated the principles of rheology and material behaviour under different forces.

Children immersed in discovery

The atmosphere was electric with the enthusiasm of young minds. Children navigated through exhibits with wide-eyed curiosity, participating in experiments that transformed theoretical concepts into tangible experiences. The hands-on nature of the demonstrations enabled them to connect classroom learning with real-world applications, making science both accessible and exciting.

Parents observed their children’s engagement with pride, noting how such experiences could spark a lifelong interest in STEM fields. The interactive environment encouraged questions and exploration, allowing children to envision themselves as future scientists and innovators.

The lasting impact on young minds

Exposure to scientific environments during the formative years plays a crucial role in shaping future aspirations. Research indicates that early engagement in science activities can significantly influence academic choices and career paths. A study evaluating out-of-school science activities for primary school children found that such experiences increased knowledge and interest in science, effects that were sustained over several years and influenced later academic choices in secondary school.

By interacting with scientists and witnessing real-world applications of scientific principles, children can see the tangible outcomes of pursuing a career in STEM. This early exposure demystifies complex subjects and builds confidence, making the prospect of a career in science or engineering more attainable.

A community united in learning

The Open Day also served as a platform for community building, bringing together individuals from diverse backgrounds with a shared interest in science and technology. Families, educators, students, and professionals mingled, exchanging ideas and fostering a collective appreciation for the role of science in society.

Such events highlight the importance of community engagement in education, demonstrating that learning extends beyond the classroom. They provide a space where curiosity is celebrated and the seeds of innovation are sown.

Conclusion

IISc Bengaluru's Open Day 2025 was more than just an exhibition; it was a celebration of knowledge, curiosity, and the endless possibilities that science offers. By opening its doors to the public, IISc not only showcased its advancements but also inspired the next generation of thinkers, creators, and problem-solvers. The lasting imprint left on the minds of young visitors is a testament to the event's success, as they carry these experiences forward into their educational journeys and future careers.

In a world where scientific literacy is increasingly vital, such initiatives play a pivotal role in nurturing informed, curious, and innovative individuals ready to tackle the challenges of tomorrow.

The samosa and ice cream stalls were also teeming with eager kids and happy parents sharing and swapping stories! What joy on their faces!

Anuradha C is an IT industry drop-out after several years of slogging and money-making. She is now working freelance as a corporate technical trainer and content writer. She is hoping to channelize her passion for writing into a satisfying experience for herself and a joyous experience for her readers. She can be reached at anuradhac@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anuradha C
IISc Bengaluru’s Open Day went beyond research – it was about sparking imaginations and bringing people together. Anuradha C shares some highlights from the event.

item.title: A mindful matter
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

In the 21st century, students seem to be constantly distracted while also having high expectations of themselves and others. In this fast-paced, technology-driven world, they face increasing pressure to succeed academically, socially, and personally. To manage these pressures effectively, cultivating mindfulness, self-compassion, and a focus on physical and mental wellbeing is essential. Mindfulness helps students stay grounded in the present moment, reducing anxiety and improving focus. By embracing self-compassion and making time for self-care, students can build resilience and lead more balanced and fulfilling lives.

Understanding mindfulness

Mindfulness is the practice of being fully aware of the present moment, observing one’s thoughts, feelings, and surroundings without judgment. This practice encourages students to live in the moment, rather than be trapped in regrets about the past or anxieties about the future. By cultivating awareness of the present, students can engage more meaningfully with their surroundings, studies, and relationships.

Mindfulness is more than simply sitting quietly in meditation. It’s about being fully present during everyday activities. Students who practice mindfulness learn to focus on what truly matters, whether they are studying, having a conversation, or participating in extracurricular activities. This focus helps them let go of distractions and negative thoughts, allowing them to manage stress more effectively and respond thoughtfully to challenging situations. By being present, students develop greater clarity and emotional stability.

Why students need mindfulness

In today’s highly competitive world, stress is unavoidable, but when that stress becomes overwhelming and toxic, it can lead to burnout and anxiety. While stress can be healthy and motivate growth, prolonged stress can hinder a student’s ability to concentrate, learn, and thrive. Practicing mindfulness helps students break free from the cycle of constant pressure and learn how to manage stress constructively.

Mindfulness helps students become more aware of their emotional responses and gain control over their reactions. Rather than being overwhelmed by negative emotions or anxious thoughts, students who practice mindfulness can observe their thoughts calmly, allowing them to respond in a more balanced way. This not only improves emotional regulation but also enhances focus, academic performance, and overall wellbeing. Students who are mindful are more capable of handling the stressors of school and life without letting their mental health derail.

How students can practice mindfulness

Mindfulness can be woven into the fabric of daily life. One simple way to practice mindfulness is by being fully engaged during conversations. When talking with someone, students should focus not only on the words being spoken but also on the other person’s tone of voice, body language, and emotions. This mindful listening helps build stronger connections and empathy, which are key to positive relationships.

Another practice is mindfulness in daily tasks, such as washing dishes or doing schoolwork. By paying attention to the sensations of these activities – the flow of water on their hands or the sound of their pencil on paper – students can remain grounded in the present moment. Whenever they notice their mind wandering, they can gently bring their attention back to what they are doing, reinforcing their ability to focus and stay calm.

The importance of self-compassion and self-care for students

In addition to mindfulness, self-compassion plays a crucial role in maintaining wellbeing. In the face of academic pressure and high expectations, students may often feel the need to be perfect, leading to burnout and stress. Self-compassion involves treating oneself with kindness, especially in moments of failure or difficulty. It encourages students to recognize that everyone makes mistakes and that perfection is not required for success.

Self-care is essential for students, who often juggle demanding academic schedules with social and extracurricular commitments. Taking time out for oneself – whether it’s by exercising, journalling, or simply resting – allows students to recharge and maintain their mental and physical health. It’s important to understand that self-care is not selfish; it is necessary for sustained wellbeing. When students take care of their minds and bodies, they become better equipped to manage the demands of school and life.

Strategies for prioritizing physical and mental wellbeing

Maintaining good physical health is key to supporting mental wellbeing. Simple practices such as drinking enough water, eating nutritious meals, exercising regularly, and getting sufficient sleep can have profound effects on a student’s overall health and ability to cope with stress. Exercise, in particular, is known to boost mood and reduce anxiety through the release of endorphins.

Students can also practice self-care by engaging in reflective activities, such as journalling or meditation. Writing down thoughts and emotions helps students process their feelings and gain perspective on their experiences. Meditation allows students to quieten their minds, focus on their breath, and cultivate a sense of inner peace. These practices, though simple, can have significant positive effects on students' ability to handle stress and maintain emotional balance.

Each student is unique, so it’s important for individuals to explore different self-care strategies and discover what works best for them. While some students may find peace through yoga or reading, others might prefer activities like drawing or walking. Finding a personal method for relaxation and stress relief is key to sustaining wellbeing throughout the challenges of school life.

Conclusion

In the ever-changing, high-pressure world of the 21st century, practicing mindfulness, self-compassion, and self-care is more important than ever for students. By staying present, observing thoughts without judgment, and nurturing their physical and mental wellbeing, students can not only reduce stress but also lead more fulfilling and successful lives. Mindfulness helps them manage their emotions and focus on what truly matters, while self-compassion encourages them to treat themselves with kindness, especially during difficult times. Prioritizing wellbeing ensures that students are equipped to handle the demands of modern life with resilience, clarity, and purpose.

Priyanka Soman has been a teacher at The School (KFI) for around six years, where she teaches music, social studies, and chemistry. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Priyanka Soman
In a fast-paced world, mindfulness helps students stay focused, manage stress, and build emotional strength. Priyanka Soman shares practical ways to integrate mindfulness into daily life.

item.title: Teaching meaning-making through personal connections
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Srishti Chandwadkar

The academic year always starts with palpable excitement, hope, and passion to do something significant in the lives of learners. It is an intense time when books are read, goals are made, resources explored, and videos watched as much as humanly possible, so that the rest of the year runs smoothly. As an ESL (English as a Second Language) educator at Shridha School, Indore, I did not know that all this work would barely be of use. It was a hard realization.

I began teaching the English language to learners with different needs. It was a rough start. They struggled with reading so much that I had to explain many words in a sentence. I used visuals for one student on the autism spectrum, but that did not work for another student who struggled with comprehension. A third student wanted to learn for 30 minutes straight, but a fourth needed a break every 20 minutes. I scratched my head trying to find ways to teach abstract adjectives and adverbs. How do you teach these things using concise and clear language? How did my teacher teach me? Do I even need to teach these words in depth? Can’t they just sight-read or understand the simpler grammar on their own? Will they even need to use it? It was then that I realized that despite identifying myself as a neurotypical human being and having a good vocabulary, I still could not communicate my emotions and needs efficiently. This brought me to their level. We all struggle with language because we have created it. And just like us, it is not perfect. There are always nuances missed, subtle hints misunderstood, words miscomprehended, phrases misused, sentences skipped, and punctuations and tonality confused. I am a learner as much as they are and I am going to teach them what I have understood about language so that they can better understand and interact with the world around them. This clarified to me the purpose of learning language, which is to understand each other through our unique and similar contexts, so that we can reach a shared understanding about our experiences of living in the world. Isn’t this why we created language in the first place?

I changed the way I was teaching vocabulary. I remember my English teachers holding the book in their hands, standing with neutral expressions on their faces, trying to build the scene with their tone and voice modulations, and asking us to cram the meanings of difficult words. The correct usage of the word was left for us to embark upon at our leisure. It was going to be challenging teaching difficult words and their usage to learners with neurodiversity.

I figured I might need more than voice modulations and verbal explanations to explain the meaning of complex words. Especially, words whose context changes with place and time. Here’s what I tried:

  1. Visuals: I added visuals, gifs, and videos to my vocabulary teaching resource, so that my students could easily see and not coerce their brain to create an imagery of an object whose name they are hearing for the first time. This not only gave them reference imagery for the word but also added to their knowledge. Below is an example of how I have used visuals to explain words.
  1. Give examples: Before asking them to share their connections, I tell them how I am connected in different ways with a particular word/words. This is to get the wheels in their brain moving. To get them thinking in different directions. For instance, while discussing the word, ‘worry’, I shared how sometimes I feel scared for my mother when she is sick, or how I am scared whether I will perform well in a test. I also share real experiences like losing my dog once while taking her on a walk and feeling so scared that I cried. Then I shared how in all these experiences I was feeling that something bad might happen and replaced the word ‘scared’ with ‘worried’.
  1. Make connections: I then asked my learners to share if they or people they knew had experienced something similar. And guess what? They had so much to share. They had experiences with the word, had seen it, used it, lived it, played with it, eaten it, etc. They had done so much with the word without knowing what it meant. At first, I had them share their observations in their preferred language and then slowly shared my connection with a particular English word in Hindi, a language they were comfortable with. This gave them enough ideas about how the word is used in a sentence. Later, I gave them sentence starters in English to start their sentences. With sufficient practice, learners began to share their connections using the new word.

Example – I would tell them, “Think of an incident when you feel scared for someone or something and just share the incident. You can try using the vocabulary word ‘worry’ – if you want.” One student shared, “My mom worry I am sick”; another shared, “My mom worries I will fall teacups”, and a third whose grandfather is sick shared, “I worry about my grandfather”, using the vocabulary word directly in a sentence.

  1. Review and repeat: Revision helps solidify learning. The human brain forgets information after a few seconds, minutes, or hours. Revising the vocabulary is a way to make sure that learning is not fading with time. Other than the widely used revision methods such as fill in the blanks, true or false, match the columns, you can also try activities such as Pictionary, where one learner draws the vocabulary word on the board and the others guess, or guess the word by the picture/gif used, fill missing letters using the hint (text or picture form), etc.

The same method of meaning-making can be applied to discussing text. Asking learners what they think about while reading a sentence and whether they have had similar experiences as in the story, makes them think about the text in personal ways. Learning becomes active when one goes beyond the textbook. Between the story and the lived experience of the learner, a connection is built of shared experiences, similar timelines, and actions helping the learner understand themselves and others better.

The importance of context, therefore, is undisputed. I strongly feel that it is vital when teaching language that we understand the context of the learner and use relevant examples from their life, settings, and people so that they can learn meaning-making on their own.

Srishti Chandwadkar is an educator with three years of experience teaching and designing curricula for English. Passionate about integrating arts into language learning, she explores how children make meaning, and connect language and thought, aiming to creatively enhance literacy skills and empower learners. In her free time she loves to read, paint, and write personal essays. She can be reached at srishti.chandwadkar@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Srishti Chandwadkar
The purpose of learning language is to understand each other through our unique or similar contexts. Srishti Chandwadkar shares teaching methods that empower neurodiverse learners to make meaning on their own terms.

item.title: Sometimes 'no' is the right answer
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anubha Rawat

Aana is a high school student balancing a rigorous academic schedule with her role as captain of the school debate club. Her class teacher asks her to lead a new community development initiative, but Aana knows she cannot manage it without sacrificing her performance in her current duties. Aana says ‘no’ and politely declines the offer.

Aana’s refusal does not go down well with her class teacher.

Sammy is a dedicated sportsman who volunteers at a local animal welfare centre. His coach asks him to extend his practice hours, which Sammy surmises will conflict with his animal care work. Sammy says ‘no’. He respectfully communicates his restrictions and the importance of his volunteer commitment.

But his coach is not willing to listen!

‘No’ often carries a heavy weight, especially in educational and social contexts. Many times, the emotional response of the adult in such situations can be quite intense. Teachers may feel disappointed or rejected, interpreting the refusal as a sign of defiance, an act of indiscipline, or a lack of respect from the student. These feelings are most often fuelled by a sense of personal investment – time and energy into guiding, motivating, and supporting their students – all done with the best intentions for students' success and a genuine desire to see them embrace opportunities.

Do you burst into a barrage of statements when a student says, “No, ma’am,” to a request of yours?

Do you feel let down or even humiliated, questioning why a student would refuse your well-meaning request or demand?

Do you experience frustration, feeling that the student's refusal undermines your efforts to engage and motivate?

Do you feel a fleeting sense of hurt, as if the student’s ‘no’ is a rejection of your guidance and support?

While these reactions are natural, educators need to recognize that the student's ‘no’ is not about challenging authority or rejecting responsibility. In fact, it reflects your student's ability to prioritize their wellbeing. It indicates that they are confidently navigating their limitations and priorities, ensuring optimal management of their life.

Fostering a balanced approach

Educators play a vital role in understanding the student’s perspective and guiding students through the nuances of saying ‘no’. By cultivating an environment of mutual respect and free-flowing, honest communication, they can help students understand when it is appropriate to assert boundaries and when it is crucial to comply with requirements. Teaching students how to strike this balance effectively can help maintain their wellbeing while following standardized guidelines and regulations.

Here are a few suggestions on how to guide students in understanding the when, why, where, what, and to whom. ‘No’ might be the appropriate answer.

When saying ‘no’ is appropriate

  • Teach students to recognize signs of being overwhelmed, such as stress, fatigue, or declining performance in their commitments. Encourage them to evaluate their present obligations and determine if they can realistically handle more tasks. When they feel stretched too thin, it is okay to decline additional requests. For instance, consider a student who has been asked to take on an additional project by their teacher. If this request comes at a time when the student is already managing a heavy workload, saying ‘no’ becomes a vital act of self-preservation, allowing the student to focus on the tasks at hand without compromising their academic performance or personal health.
  • In cases where students get distracted from their academic goals or personal growth, saying ‘no’ can be a legitimate reason to stay focussed. For example, if a student is invited to an event that could distract from their preparation for an important exam, a respectful refusal can help.

Why saying ‘no’ is important

  • Explain that saying ‘no’ helps maintain their mental and emotional health by preventing burnout and overexertion. It allows them to manage stress and focus on what truly matters.
  • It is equally important for students to establish boundaries to protect their personal time and mental health. Discuss how saying ‘no’ helps them honour their needs and priorities. It’s a way to assert their values and parameters.
  • Highlight that making thoughtful decisions about when to say ‘no’ enhances their ability to evaluate their capabilities and make informed choices.

Where to say ‘no’

  • Students might need to say ‘no’ to extra projects or activities that conflict with their academic requirements. Teach them to communicate this respectfully to their teachers.
  • Students should understand that it is okay to say ‘no’ to social invitations or peer pressures that do not align with their beliefs or wellbeing.
  • Students can say ‘no’ to additional duties in sports, clubs, or other activities if they interfere with their overall balance and performance.

What to say ‘no’ to

  • Guide students in identifying when a request or demand is unreasonable or beyond their capacity. It is important to know when to decline such requests.
  • Help students understand that there is nothing wrong in saying ‘no’ to tasks that distract them from their primary goals or activities that go against their value system or cause discomfort.

Whom to say ‘no’ to

  • Teach students how to say ‘no’ to additional responsibilities or requests from teachers and coaches, explaining their current commitments or constraints clearly.
  • Encourage students to assertively say ‘no’ to friends or peers who may pressurize them into activities or decisions they are not comfortable with.
  • Explain that it is also acceptable to define boundaries with family members when students are asked to take on additional tasks that they cannot manage.

When saying ‘no’ is inappropriate

  • Students need to understand that certain responsibilities, such as writing exams or completing required coursework, cannot be declined. Saying ‘no’ to these mandatory assignments undermines the educational process and can negatively impact their academic progress.
  • Backing out of commitments without a valid explanation can hurt relationships and trust.
  • Students should also be encouraged to face challenges and seize learning opportunities, even if they are initially reluctant. Refusing to participate in challenging activities out of fear or discomfort can hinder personal and academic growth.
  • Saying ‘no’ in a way that trivializes or disrespects someone’s genuine efforts or feelings. For example, a child bluntly and rudely turns down a teacher’s well-intentioned advice when it is for his own good.
  • Impulsively saying ‘no’ without understanding the full context of the request, such as dismissing a suggestion in a group activity, because it comes from someone you disagree with.

Practical strategies for teaching students to say ‘no’

Developing the skill of saying ‘no’ involves practical strategies and clear examples that students can relate to and apply. Here’s how to help them develop this skill constructively:

  • Role-playing exercises: Use role-playing to practice saying ‘no’ in various scenarios. This helps students feel more confident and prepared for real-life situations.
  • Discussing real-life examples: Share stories or examples of individuals who effectively lay down rules and the resulting positive outcomes.
  • Creating a safe space: Foster an environment where students feel safe to express their limitations without fear of judgment or retribution.
  • Encouraging open communication: Teach students how to communicate their ‘no’ firmly and respectfully, offering explanations when required. For example, a student declining an additional project might say, ‘I appreciate the offer, but presently I am committed to other tasks and need to focus on them to perform well.’

After practising saying ‘no’, discuss how it felt, what went well, and what could be improved. This reflection helps students see the benefits, learn from their experiences, and develop better decision-making skills.

Saying ‘no’ is a valuable life skill! It is a necessary and sometimes empowering choice that students must navigate carefully. By fostering an understanding of when to set boundaries and when to adhere to rules and responsibilities, educators can help students thrive both academically and personally!

Anubha Rawat is a dedicated educator, known for her engaging pedagogical style, and is passionate about creating vibrant and positive learning spaces for young minds to foster a love for the English language. Her published works are available on Amazon, including her latest book, Scrolls of Teaching Penned by Students, a delightful compilation of insights gained from classroom experiences. She can be reached at aana1924@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anubha Rawat
When Aana declined her teacher’s request, and Sammy said “no” to his coach, both adults reacted poorly. Here’s why Anubha Rawat believes that teaching students to say “no” appropriately is vital.

item.title: A slide down the mountains
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nimesh Ved

What does this book talk about?

This book talks about the mountain ranges across the length and breadth of India – from Karakoram to the Eastern Ghats and from the Aravalli to the Naga Hills. It takes the readers along these ranges and provides information on the types of mountains, their history, and the species that inhabit the mountains. It also clarifies terms and concepts pertaining to mountains. Black and white maps, illustrations, and images in different sizes are present in generous numbers across the book. Towards the end, it also contains “mountain notes” on the mountain railways in India, mountain music, how we can conserve our mountains, etc.

What I enjoyed while reading this book?

The book clarifies commonly used words related to mountains. This is very helpful, given that many a time, we end up using words without adequately understanding them. It tells how the “fault-blocked mountains” are different from the “fold mountains”, or that the coniferous forests have “tall cone-bearing trees like pine”. It also brings out facts that we may have overlooked – that Nanda Devi at 7,817 metres, for example, is fully in India, while Kanchenjunga at 8,598 metres is in India and Nepal.

Similarly, the maps depicting the locations of the mountain ranges across the country and the timeline specifying their ages are useful too. The text is light and is peppered with generous doses of humour. The parallels drawn in the book too are of the kind that can resonate well with the young – those of recent movies, for example.

What in the book did not work for me?

This book has a very utilitarian take on the mountains. In the initial pages itself mountains are to “supply freshwater”, “provide food”, or “blocking mountain winds”. Should one not love the mountains just because they are there?

The tone is distant, western, urban, and elitist. It glorifies what researchers, scientists, geologists, climbers, trekkers, and academics have done or can do. The book talks about mountains without mentioning the people of the mountains, their food, their stories, their songs, their wisdom, their clothes, their way of life, and all else. There are fleeting glimpses, but overall, the people are conspicuous by their absence.

The book focuses on wildlife. However, there are points where research could have been better – the lions have not been reintroduced in Kumbhalgarh, but the book seems to indicate differently. There are repeated mentions of wildlife living in national parks, but there is no talk about the animals living there using the larger mountainous landscapes, of which protected areas are a small part. And, the layout could have been better too. It leads to confusion in some places like when it appears to discuss Loktak lake as part of Meghalaya or when it seems to point that the hoolock gibbons are found only in Manipur.

The book looks at the world in black and white. The nuances are kept at bay,as over-simplification creeps in. This line for example, “Should governments cater to people’s needs, or should governments leave the mountains untouched?” At multiple places, the book points out that threats (to the mountains) are from people, but does not venture further – whether these are from policymakers, industrialists, locals, tourists, or others.

The book spreads itself thin. I wonder what the point was in talking about our independence movement or showing the images of defense personnel practicing yoga on the mountains. This brings me to the images and illustrations. They do not work either, especially the small-sized ones.

The book appears like a collage of information on mountains. A well-intentioned effort that tries a bit too much and ends up achieving little. There are books that take the reader to the mountains and make them fall in love with mountains. This book is not one of them.

Nimesh Ved loves walking, cycling, and is often seen amidst books. He enjoys putting his thoughts in words. He blogs at https://nimesh-ved.blogspot.com/. He can be reached at nimesh.explore@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nimesh Ved
“Should one not love the mountains because they are there?” Nimesh Ved reviews Mala Kumar’s “Up the Mountains of India.”

item.title: The math connection in a global world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

In today's world, everything is connected. Technology lets us talk to people across the globe, and events in one country can affect the whole world. The old way of teaching, where every student learns the same thing in the same way, does not work anymore. Students come from different cultures, speak different languages, and have different experiences. Schools need to change to fit this diversity and teach skills that help students understand this new changing world.

Schools should be flexible and make learning meaningful for all students. To do this, we must focus on global skills – such as understanding different cultures, working with others, and solving real-world problems.

The global mindset in the classroom

You might not expect global learning to happen in a math class, but math can be a great way to explore world issues. Reimers (2009)[*] explains that global skills are not just for history or geography; they are useful in all subjects, including math. "Global competency is the knowledge and skills people need to understand the world and find solutions to problems," he says.

Math is often seen as a set of numbers and rules, but it actually connects with many real-world topics. By linking math lessons to global issues, teachers can help students see why math matters beyond the classroom.

Math lessons with a global focus

Algebra: Understanding income and fair pay around the world

  • Compare salaries in different countries.
  • Analyze cost-of-living differences.
  • Use equations to understand affordability of basic needs.

Percentages: Comparing energy use across countries

  • Investigate different countries' energy sources (solar, wind, fossil fuels).
  • Calculate percentages of renewable versus non-renewable energy use.
  • Explore sustainable energy solutions.

Measurement: Comparing water usage around the world

  • Measure the daily use of water in your house.
  • Find similar data for your country.
  • Compare this with water use in other countries.
  • Discuss ways to save water.

Addition and Subtraction: Sharing resources fairly

  • Use real-life scenarios like distributing food or books.
  • Create word problems that focus on fairness.
  • Discuss how different communities share resources.

Shapes and Patterns: Cultural designs in art and architecture

  • Explore the kinds of patterns used in traditional crafts from different countries.
  • Identify and compare shapes used in buildings worldwide.
  • Create art inspired by global designs.

Money and Currency: Understanding world currencies

  • Compare values of different currencies.
  • Solve problems related to currency conversion.
  • Discuss how exchange rates affect global trade.

Assessing global thinking in math

To check if students are developing a global mindset, teachers should look at more than just the math. They should also see how well students understand the bigger picture.

For example, for the measurement lesson on water usage, assessment methods can include:

  • Calculations of daily water use (math skills).
  • A reflection on differences in water access across countries (critical thinking).
  • A simple plan for reducing water waste at home (problem-solving).

Global mindset: a path to lifelong learning

A few years ago, I taught a group of students who didn’t enjoy math. One day, I showed them how math is used to track endangered animals. They researched different species, collected data, and made graphs. Suddenly, they were excited about numbers because they saw how math could help the world.

This proves that education doesn’t have to change completely – it just needs to connect to real life. By making lessons meaningful and including global topics, we can help students become curious, thoughtful, and ready to make a difference in the world.

Reimers (2009) believes that when teachers bring global education into the classroom, it can create a powerful change. By helping students see how subjects like math connect to real-world problems, we prepare them not just for exams, but for life as global citizens.

[*]Reimers, F. (2009). Global competency: The knowledge and skills people need to understand the world and find solutions to problems.

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Could statistics help children understand climate change? Monica Kochar reveals how mathematics becomes a powerful tool for developing global awareness, critical thinking, and problem-solving.
